channel.title: Teacher Plus
channel.description: The magazine for the contemporary teacher

item.title: What we miss when we make it easy
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
From the end of the year to the beginning of the next, across the country, it’s the season of literary and cultural festivals. From the celebration of music and dance that is the Marghazhi Vizha of Chennai to the carefully curated assemblage of art that is the Kochi Biennale, and the scores of literary gatherings in cities as far apart as Jaipur and Kolkata, Patna and Hyderabad, Shillong and Mumbai, there’s no dearth of opportunities to travel through the cultural landscape, no matter what your taste.
 
Most literary and arts festivals have something for children too: author talks, art workshops, and interactive storytelling, for instance. So, one increasingly sees whole families make an outing of it, giving children the opportunity to browse, listen to people talk about books and reading, and engage in non-routine activities that are at once fun and instructive. At the Hyderabad Literary Festival, for instance, I saw a child pick up a book of poetry and approach the author to sign her copy, and another proudly wave around a cartoon he had drawn. Some sat in sessions, quietly listening, while others walked through exhibitions and art installations with wide eyes, not quite understanding, perhaps, but taking it all in. Among the many things literary and cultural festivals can do, one is to spark curiosity and see where the pursuit of an idea can lead. Children watch and listen to successful people who have followed their curiosity and turned it into art and story, they see how sticking to a passion or making a commitment to one’s ideals can take you places, and possibly, they get a sense of the work that goes into it all.
 
There’s a short poem doing the rounds on social media, titled For a student who used AI to write a paper that asks the question, “what are you trying to be free of?” and ends with the line: “Love is for the ones who love the work.” It’s something many of us teachers have wondered about: what exactly are kids doing with the time they purportedly save by taking technology-enabled shortcuts? This was the question that led us to the theme of this month’s cover story – why has hard work now become so difficult to commit to? And what are we missing in the process of learning when we are forced to make everything easy? Why should engagement be the burden a teacher must carry, rather than something that is fostered in the learner, driven by an energy that they bring to the task? These are the questions that Mahak Khanna puzzles through in her article, questions that we have all asked so often, more so in recent times, when we have needed to always think about ways to harness and hold children’s attention.
 
Taking children to events such as literary and cultural festivals may be one way to get them to re-focus, in a non-instrumental way, on the joy and pain of creation, prompting the understanding that it is the tough work-out that builds muscle, that it is the revisions that make the masterpiece. That shortcuts may make things easy, but they rarely make things shine.

item.title: A real-world spin on the five laws of library science
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Satakshi Sharma

School libraries are not just shelves of books; they are spaces that can spark children’s curiosity and support learning. Dr. S. R. Ranganathan, regarded as the father of library science in India, introduced the Five Laws of Library Science to promote user-centred and effective libraries. As an English teacher, my classroom experiences and interactions with students inspired this exploration of his ideas. This article examines Ranganathan’s Five Laws and connects them with essential soft skills such as listening, empathy, and flexibility to strengthen the role of school libraries.

How we tackled it

We went with a qualitative approach, focusing on real stories. We interviewed school librarians, watched libraries in action, and got feedback from students and teachers through surveys. Then we looked for patterns to link soft skills to each of the Five Laws.

Breaking it down

  • Law 1: Books are for use: This one’s about making books easy to reach. It takes initiative and responsibility to set up a space that children want to hang out in. Picture a library where the shelves are arranged so anyone can find what they need fast.
  • Law 2: Every reader his/her book: This is about finding the right book for each child, no matter their interests or reading level. Empathy and listening are key. For example, a librarian might notice a shy reader loves comics and suggest the perfect graphic novel.
  • Law 3: Every book its reader: Some books sit lonely on the shelf, so creativity and communication help them get noticed. Think colourful book displays or fun reading challenges that make children curious about new titles.
  • Law 4: Save the reader’s time: Nobody likes wasting time hunting for a book. Clear signs, smart systems, and quick help make things smooth. Time management and organization are important here, like setting up an online catalogue.
  • Law 5: The library is a growing organism: Libraries have to keep up with new tech and changes. Staying adaptable and eager to learn keeps things fresh, like adding e-books or librarians picking up new skills at workshops.

Change can be tough. Some librarians might not vibe with new ideas, or there is no training for soft skills. Budgets are often tight and small projects can be hard to scale up.Run workshops to help librarians and staff level up their soft skills. Start programs where students can be library leaders. Weave library skills and life skills into classes. Get school leaders to support these ideas with clear policies.

Soft skills add heart to school libraries, making them places that children love to explore. Libraries are always changing, and mixing these skills with the Five Laws can create a special space. It is about blending the structure of library science with the warmth of human connection.

Satakshi Sharma works at Bharatiya Vidya Bhavan Vidyashram Pratapnagar, Jaipur. She can be reached at bvb.sa@jprvidyashramprtp.ac.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Satakshi Sharma
Libraries should be inviting and engaging for children to want to come and explore them. Here’s how you can combine the Five Laws of library science with soft skills to make your school library the most happening hang out among the students.

item.title: Home away from home
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Divya Mishra

It was the first day of the new academic year. A child in a torn uniform had beaten up a few children. He was hiding under the table, looking for a safe place, where he would be heard. He was seeking someone who would listen to him, someone with whom he could share his feelings. Instead of asking him to come out, I went under the table to talk to him and said, "You know what, this is the safest place I have ever seen." I will never forget his expression; he was relieved as if he were in the safest place with someone who was thinking just like him.

That day marked the beginning of a relationship where he never hesitated to talk to me about anything he felt, liked, or hated. 

He struggled with his academics and had a hard time with his friends because of his behaviour, but still, he became a child I trusted. I told him that he was special to me, that he would have friends, and that he could reach high. 

He became so close to me that he started enjoying school. His parents and I worked hard to provide him with a safe environment, both at home and at school. He began to improve and behave well. Now, he is one of the top performers, excelling academically, and is a good classical singer too. 

Building a safe space for children in classrooms not only enriches the classroom, but also changes their lives altogether. A safe environment not only helps facilitators finish their lessons on time and achieve maximum academic success, but it also empowers children to express their thoughts confidently for the rest of their lives. 

Is creating a safe environment a difficult job?  

The answer to this question is certainly yes, as you need to allow children the freedom to express what they want to speak about, whether it's having lunch in the first period or taking a nap before the lesson starts. Now, returning to the same question, is it really difficult? The answer lies in how much control you can let go of, so that you can think about the more important aspects. 

At Aarambh Preschool, we believe that every child should have the confidence to communicate with adults and speak fearlessly. And we are able to make this happen because we provide a free, non-judgmental space to share, whether it involves questioning management decisions or breaking stereotypes. In this journey of more than a decade, I have learned that the amount of free and safe environment you create for teachers automatically reflects in the foundation of the organization, which is the children. 

We, as leaders, should take responsibility for teachers, as they are the ones who will handle children with care, just as we handle teachers with love and care.

Is social-emotional learning important?

Social-emotional learning plays a key role in building such an environment, often involving storytelling. One thing I believe in is the power of listening. The ability to listen is very low these days, as no one seems to have the time.

Children have so much to express, especially when they are struggling to understand their emotions. As teachers, it is important to provide them with the space to share what they want, assured that they will be heard.

Empathy is one important aspect that stands out the most for me. It's hard to imagine what children experience at every age; if we empathize, understand, and help them, we can make a difference.

Sometimes we tend to focus on the things that we can always see, but we forget to see those things that are not visible. We work on the behaviour of the child, but we don't try to understand the why behind the behaviour. It's time to focus on such intangible things. This can only happen when you are emotionally connected to each child. The bond that a teacher forms with their students can push the child's limits.

Imagine a child discovering a safe place to learn and share, creating a home away from home for them.

Divya Mishra is Academic Head, Aarambh Preschool, Raipur, Chhattisgarh. She can be reached at divya@aarambhschool.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Divya Mishra
Children, young or old, need a space where they feel safe, safe enough to express themselves, ask questions, and share without fear. In such spaces, children will blossom into complete individuals. As adults we have to put in the effort to make our schools and classrooms such places for our children.

item.title: Making science come alive
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Sravanthi Datla

As teachers we often face the challenge of making abstract scientific concepts clear and memorable for our students. Topics like eclipses can, especially, be tricky. Diagrams in textbooks explain the mechanics, but for many children, they remain flat and confusing. I wanted to find a way to make the lesson more engaging and ensure that students not only understood but also remembered it. That’s when I turned to drama and storytelling.

The story idea

I framed the lesson as a short story in which three powerful celestial friends — the Sun, the Moon, and the Earth — play a game of hide-and-seek in the sky. To bring this alive, I invited three students to enact the roles while I narrated the story. Immediately, the classroom atmosphere changed. What could have been a routine explanation became a small theatre performance with the rest of the class watching intently.

Scene 1: The solar eclipse

Moon stepped in front of Sun and spread out their hands dramatically. Earth looked up and exclaimed, “My skies are darkening in the middle of the day!” Sun looked at Moon and said, “You little prankster, let me shine again soon.”

Laughter rippled through the classroom, but at the same time, the scientific point became clear: a solar eclipse occurs when the moon moves between the sun and the earth, blocking sunlight for a brief period. Only those standing in the moon’s shadow can see it.

Scene 2: The lunar eclipse

Next, it was Earth’s turn to move between Sun and Moon. As Earth slowly blocked Sun’s rays, Moon acted out in confusion — “Hey! I’m turning red. What’s happening?”

The narrator explained how the earth’s atmosphere bends some sunlight, giving the moon a reddish glow during a lunar eclipse. This performance helped the students grasp why lunar eclipses look different from solar eclipses and why more people can see them.

How students responded

The energy in the room was remarkable. Even students who are usually shy or reserved wanted to participate, either in the enactment or in the lively discussion that followed. Many asked thoughtful questions: Why doesn’t an eclipse happen every month? Can we see both eclipses from anywhere on earth? The enactment had sparked curiosity, which led to a deeper exploration of the topic.

When I asked the class to explain the difference between solar and lunar eclipses, almost everyone answered confidently. A week later, I was delighted to see that they still recalled the concepts and even recreated the mini-drama on their own. The playful performance had made the learning stick.

Why drama works in science?

Drama turns a lesson from passive listening into active participation. Students are not just hearing about a concept — they are seeing it, feeling it, and sometimes even embodying it. The combination of movement, dialogue, and imagination helps anchor new knowledge in their memory.

Science is full of processes that are invisible to the naked eye: atomic movement, planetary motion, and energy transfer. Drama allows us to make these processes visible in the classroom. More importantly, it makes the learning joyful. When students laugh, act, and engage emotionally, they remember far more than when they only copy diagrams into their notebooks.

Conclusion

My eclipse lesson reinforced my belief that drama integration can transform science teaching. With just a simple story and three student volunteers, I was able to create a lesson that was lively, interactive, and effective. The children not only enjoyed themselves but also gained a clear and lasting understanding of solar and lunar eclipses.

Drama in the classroom does not require elaborate props or scripts. Even small enactments can make big differences in how students connect with science. As teachers, if we can bring creativity and storytelling into our practice, we can make learning both meaningful and memorable.

Try this in your classroom

  • Use props: A torch (sun), a large ball (earth), and a smaller ball (moon) can help students visualize how shadows form during eclipses.
  • Role rotation: After one group enacts the drama, invite another group to take over — this ensures every child gets to participate.
  • Extend the story: Ask students to script their own dialogues for Sun, Moon, and Earth, or even create a sequel — “What happens if other planets join the game?”
  • Link to art: Encourage students to draw posters of solar and lunar eclipses after the activity, reinforcing their learning through visual expression.

Sravanthi Datla teaches science and is the Primary Coordinator at Westberry School, Bhimavaram, Andhra Pradesh. She can be reached at sravanthi.datla@westberry.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sravanthi Datla
Science is considered a practical subject, hands-on, as many concepts can be taught through experiments. But not all science can be taught through experiments. Here is where storytelling comes in handy. Create and act out stories to learn scientific processes that are invisible to the naked eye.

item.title: Teaching festivals using puppets
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
K Sasi Sowjanya

Festivals are an essential part of Indian culture, reflecting our diversity, values, and traditions. But how do we move beyond rote textbook learning to help children experience their essence? I explored this challenge through an engaging puppet story in my grade 3 classroom.

Once upon a time, there was a mother puppet named Festo - a warm, wise woman who loved celebrating festivals. She had three children:

  • Natio, who loved his country and stood tall with the Indian flag.
  • Harvi, who loved harvests and helped with preparations during Pongal and Onam.
  • Religi, who found joy in worship, lights, and spiritual songs during Diwali, Eid, and Christmas.

Each of her children represented a type of Indian festival:

  • Natio -> National Festivals (Independence Day, Republic Day)
  • Harvi -> Harvest Festivals (Pongal, Bihu, Onam, Lohri)
  • Religi -> Religious Festivals (Diwali, Eid, Christmas, Gurpurab)

As the puppets spoke and acted in class, students easily identified festival types, their values, and how they connect with real life.

Classroom integration activities

  • Costume day: Children dressed as Natio, Harvi, and Religi and shared stories about their favourite festivals.
  • Puppet-making workshop: Students created their own festival puppets with paper and cloth.
  • Festival tree chart: We made a chart showing how different festivals fit under the three categories.
  • Reflection journal: Students wrote or drew their favourite puppet and what they learned.

Impact

This story-based approach helped children understand why we celebrate festivals, not just what we celebrate. It also encouraged collaboration, creativity, and emotional connection.

References

  1. NCERT Grade 3 Social Studies Curriculum
  • Getting Ahead in Social Studies textbook (used in class)
  • National Portal of India (https://www.india.gov.in)

K. Sasi Sowjanya is a primary social studies educator in Westberry High School, Bhimavaram with a passion for creative and experiential learning. She integrates storytelling, drama, and puppetry into her lessons to make learning meaningful and joyful for young learners.She can be reached at sasi.sowjanya@westberry.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
K Sasi Sowjanya
Festivals are a big part of Indian culture and it is important that our children know about them. Yes, there are many books in the market to teach children about our different festivals, but is there another way to involve children in a lesson on festivals?

item.title: The architecture of effort: teaching the value of hard work in an age of distractions
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Mahak Khanna

In a middle-school classroom in suburban Pune, a teacher introduces a complex geometry problem. Before she can even finish drawing the diagram, a hand shoots up. It isn’t a question; it’s a surrender.

“Ma’am, I don’t get it. This is too hard,” the student says, leaning back as if the difficulty of the task were a physical barrier.

In another classroom, this one a rural government school in Telangana, the "fidget factor" has taken over during a ten-minute silent reading session. Within three minutes, pencils are being sharpened for the third time, water bottles are being refilled, and eyes are gazing the ceiling.

These scenes, recurring daily from high-fee private schools to under-resourced municipal ones, point to a deeper shift in student psyche. We are witnessing what researchers call the erosion of "cognitive stamina." Today’s students are not less intelligent than previous generations, but they are increasingly effort-intolerant. In an era where every desire is met with an immediate click – whether it’s food delivery, a YouTube video, or a Google answer, the slow, often frustrating, process of learning feels like a system error.

The "algorithm of ease" vs. the "labour of learning"

To address this, we must first understand the environment that shapes our students. Modern childhood is lived within the attention economy. According to the ASER 2024 report, nearly 90% of rural teenagers (ages 14-16) now use smartphones fluently, yet 76% use them primarily for social media rather than education.

Platforms like Instagram and YouTube are engineered using variable reward schedules – the same psychological mechanism used in slot machines. Every swipe provides a micro-dose of dopamine. Recent 2026 studies indicate that the average human attention span has declined to roughly 8.25 seconds, while Gen Z users toggle between apps every 44 seconds.

When these students enter a classroom, they encounter "friction." Learning, real, deep learning, is inherently high-friction. It requires holding multiple variables in the mind, tolerating confusion, and revising work without an immediate "high-score." To a brain conditioned by the algorithm of ease, a difficult math problem isn't just a challenge; it feels like an affront to their comfort.

The Indian context: the marks vs. mastery paradox

In India, the struggle to teach the value of effort is complicated by our high-stakes testing culture. For decades, hard work in an Indian household meant manan (contemplation) and abhyas (practice). However, in the race for board exam percentages, hard work has been narrowed down to high-volume rote memorization.

When we tell a student to "work hard," they often hear "spend more hours memorizing." This transactional view of effort, doing work only for a specific grade, actually weakens their intrinsic motivation. Furthermore, the rise of shadow education (tuition culture) has unintentionally lowered the threshold for effort. If a student finds a concept difficult in school, they don't wrestle with it; they wait for their home tutor to simplify it. We have created a safety net that prevents students from ever falling into – and climbing out of – the "pit of learning."

A powerful shift in Indian pedagogy is happening through the lens of Productive Failure (PF). At Sunbeam English School, Bhagwanpur (Varanasi), teachers have experimented with intentionally allowing students to struggle with complex problems before beginning formal instruction.

In a recent science lesson on filtration, instead of lecturing on the process, the teacher provided various materials (sand, charcoal, cloth) and asked students to design a filter. Most failed. Their water remained murky. However, when the teacher finally assembled the lesson, the students were leaning in. Because they had felt the frustration of failure, they were cognitively primed to understand the why behind the correct filtration sequence. They learned that the hard work of the failed attempt was actually the most important part of the lesson.

At SAI International School in Odisha, teachers use a Thinking Journal to de-stigmatize the struggle. When a class VII student hits a wall in geometry, the teacher doesn't rescue them. Instead, they ask: "What strategy worked for a similar problem last week?" or "Could your group sketch three different possibilities before I give you a hint?"

By documenting wrong turns in their journals, students at SAI learn that the struggle isn't an accident; it's a carefully prepared ground where confidence takes root. As cognitive specialists at the school note, the goal is to transform the fear of "I can't do this" into the empowered mindset of "I haven't solved this yet."

Deep dive: why boredom is fertilizer for effort

To truly teach the value of effort, we must help students reclaim the lost art of being bored. In the modern Indian household, the moment a child expresses boredom, a screen is offered as a digital pacifier. However, neuroscientists suggest that boredom is the precursor to creativity. When the brain is not being stimulated externally, it enters the Default Mode Network (DMN). This is where we reflect, problem-solve, and develop the internal drive to create our own work.

By removing all "friction" from a child’s life, we prevent their prefrontal cortex – the part of the brain responsible for impulse control – from maturing. Hard work requires executive function. If a child never has to wait for a toy or spend a quiet afternoon without a gadget, they do not develop the neural pathways required to sit through a complex 45-minute Algebra lesson. As educators, we must advocate for low-stimulation zones, explaining to parents that a bored child is a child on the verge of an intellectual breakthrough.

Strategies for the classroom: building the "effort muscle"

If hard work is a skill, we must teach it with the same intentionality as we teach grammar. Here are five "effort coaching" strategies to try:

1. The ten-minute deep work rule

Instead of asking for 40 minutes of attention, use the Pomodoro approach. Tell students: "For the next 12 minutes, we are entering a 'deep work zone.' No questions, no washroom breaks, just you and your notebook." Gradually increase this duration. This builds attention stamina.

2. The no-eraser week

Students must strike out mistakes rather than erase them. This makes the history of their effort visible. It teaches them that the scribbles and the wrong turns are the evidence of a working mind.

3. The 3-2-1 revision protocol

After a first draft of an essay, students must find 3 things they want to improve, 2 mistakes they discovered themselves, and 1 new idea they want to add. This prevents the "one and done" mentality.

4. The help ticket

Before asking a teacher for help, a student must fill out a ticket stating: "I have tried [X] and [Y], and I am stuck specifically on [Z]." This forces the student to exert effort before seeking an exit.

5. The mistake of the week

Dedicate a corner of your bulletin board to "Beautiful Mistakes." Encourage students to pin up a moment where they failed, what they learned, and how they persisted.

The role of humanities: narratives of persistence

The humanities classroom offers a unique laboratory for building perseverance. When a student reads a biography of Srinivasa Ramanujan, the focus shouldn't just be on his "genius," but on the process of his struggle. He was labelled a "slow learner" in other subjects and faced immense social challenges, yet he spent years in solitary contemplation of numbers.

By analyzing the behind-the-scenes effort of historical icons, students stop seeing success as a lightning bolt of luck and start seeing it as a series of deliberate, often exhausting, choices. This "narrative approach" helps internalize the value of struggle through empathy.

The shadow education problem: reclaiming autonomy

We must address the elephant in the Indian classroom: the tuition center. When students spend their evenings having concepts pre-digested for them by tutors, they arrive at school with a false sense of security. They "know" the answer, but they haven't "learned" it.

We can counter this by introducing invisible lessons. These are tasks where the answer is not in the textbook and cannot be Googled easily – tasks like interviewing a local artisan or observing the shadow of a flagpole every hour. These activities require autonomy and unstructured effort, qualities that are often stripped away by the over-structured life of a modern student.

The stamina challenge

The challenge: Can your class reach Gold Status in attention?

  • Week 1 (Bronze): 5 minutes of focused, silent work.
  • Week 2 (Silver): 8 minutes of focused work.
  • Week 3 (Gold): 12 minutes of "Deep Work."
  • The reward: A no-homework Friday or a class-led discussion on a topic of their choice.The reward should be for the effort of focus, not for the correctness of the work.

We cannot solve this in the classroom alone. During Parent-Teacher Meetings (PTMs), move the conversation away from marks. Ask parents: "How long can your child sit with a difficult task at home before asking for help?"

Encourage parents to:

  • Allow productive boredom. Children don't need to be entertained every second.
  • Stop over-scheduling. A child who is rushed from school to tuition to swimming has no mental energy left for deep effort.
  • Model struggle. Parents should talk about the hard things they do at work and how they persist.

Teaching the value of hard work is not about promoting the "hustle culture." In fact, chronic exhaustion is the enemy of sustained effort. We must teach students that deep work requires deep rest. Cognitive science shows that the brain consolidates learning during diffuse thinking – when we are walking, resting, or even bored.

As teachers, we must model this balance. If we are perpetually exhausted, we cannot be effective effort coaches. We must show students that persistence is a marathon, not a sprint, and that knowing when to step back is just as important as knowing when to push forward.

Teaching a child to work hard in 2026 is a subversive act. You are fighting against multibillion-dollar algorithms designed to keep them distracted and passive. You are going against a societal tide that demands instant results.

But every time you encourage a student to stay in the "learning pit" for one minute longer, you are building a muscle that will serve them long after they have forgotten the chemical formula for benzene. You are teaching them that they have agency. You are teaching them that they are not helpless in the face of difficulty.

The challenge for the Indian educator today is not just to complete the syllabus. It is to design a classroom culture where effort is honoured, where struggle is normalized, and where hard work is rediscovered as the most rewarding adventure a human mind can undertake.

Reflection questions for the staffroom

  1. Do I intervene too quickly when I see a student struggling? How can I increase my own waiting time?
  2. Does my classroom environment celebrate the final product or the messy process?
  3. How can I involve parents in valuing the struggle rather than just the score?

References

  1. Smartphone Overuse in Children in India Risks - Vision Enrich
  2. Why Indian Schools Need A Robust Digital Wellbeing Policy
  3. Impact of Distractions On Indian Students | PDF | Mental Health | Behavioural Sciences

Mahak Khanna is a creative storyteller and instructional designer with a passion for crafting engaging learning experiences and imaginative children’s stories. She loves to blend creativity with clarity to inspire young minds and adult learners alike. She can be reached at mahakkatyal18@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Mahak Khanna
How do children really learn in the age of instant answers and endless scrolling? Mahak Khanna writes about how distractions, tuition culture, and testing have reshaped students’ relationship with hard work.

item.title: Rethinking the obsession with school rankings
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Anuradha C

School rankings today command enormous attention. Released with fanfare through surveys, magazines, and digital platforms, they promise clarity in an otherwise complex education landscape. For parents seeking the “best” school and for institutions eager for recognition, these rankings have become influential markers of prestige. Yet, behind the glittering lists and celebratory banners lies a more nuanced reality – one that deserves closer scrutiny.

Rankings are not inherently problematic. But, when they become proxies for educational quality, without sufficient context or depth, they risk distorting priorities for schools, parents, and even policymakers.

What school rankings get right

At their best, school rankings offer visibility and comparison. They provide parents – especially those new to a city or education system – with a starting point. Rankings can highlight schools that demonstrate consistent academic performance and investment in student wellbeing or innovative practices. For schools, rankings can act as mirrors, prompting reflection on outcomes, infrastructure, and processes.

They also encourage accountability. When schools know they are being measured, there is an incentive to improve their systems, document outcomes, and articulate their educational philosophy more clearly. In this sense, rankings can stimulate healthy conversations around quality and standards.

Where the shine begins to mislead

The challenge arises when rankings simplify a deeply human, relational process into scores and positions. Education does not lend itself easily to league tables. Teacher engagement, student curiosity, emotional safety, and classroom culture are difficult to quantify, yet they form the bedrock of meaningful learning.

Increasingly, ranking methodologies place visible and marketable factors at the forefront – campus size, high-end infrastructure, technology displays, international collaborations, and brand recall. As a result, glitzy interiors and impressive facilities can overshadow quieter but more critical indicators such as teacher competence, mentoring systems, curriculum coherence, and inclusivity.

A beautifully designed school lobby does not guarantee inspired teaching. Nor does a high-tech classroom automatically foster critical thinking or empathy. When perception outweighs pedagogy, form begins to triumph over substance.

The fee-quality misnomer

Closely linked to rankings is another widespread assumption: higher fees mean better education. While well-funded schools may offer better infrastructure or extracurricular exposure, fees alone are not a reliable indicator of educational quality. Exceptional teaching, thoughtful leadership, and a strong learning culture are not always correlated with premium pricing.

In fact, some schools operating with modest resources demonstrate extraordinary commitment to student development, teacher training, and community engagement. Rankings that indirectly reward fee levels or visible affluence risk reinforcing the myth that education quality can be bought rather than cultivated.

Lobbying, visibility, and the ranking ecosystem

Not all ranking exercises operate with the same rigour. Some rely heavily on self-reported data, perception surveys, or paid participation models. Schools with stronger marketing budgets can invest in public relations, glossy submissions, and sustained visibility campaigns. Inclusion in ranking lists, awards, and “top school” features can sometimes owe as much to lobbying and brand positioning as to classroom realities.

This does not invalidate all rankings – but it does call for caution. Transparency of methodology, independent verification, and clarity on evaluation criteria are essential if rankings are to retain credibility.

The parental role: beyond online research

In an age of search engines and social media reviews, parents often rely heavily on online research. While this information is useful, it is incomplete. Choosing a school is too important a decision to be based solely on rankings, websites, and promotional videos.

Parents would benefit enormously from first-hand engagement – speaking directly with teachers, interacting with students, observing classrooms, and understanding daily routines. Conversations with current parents can reveal far more about a school’s culture than any ranking list. How approachable are teachers? Do students feel heard? How does the school respond to diverse learning needs? These insights rarely feature in surveys but matter deeply in lived experience.

Proximity over prestige

Another overlooked but practical consideration is distance. A highly ranked, glamorous school located 1.5 hours away may appear attractive on paper, but long commutes take a tangible toll on children and families. Fatigue, reduced playtime, limited community bonding, and daily stress can quietly erode wellbeing.

Often, a good school closer home – one that aligns reasonably well with a family’s values and offers a supportive environment – can serve a child far better than a distant, prestigious institution. Familiarity with the local community, friendships in the neighbourhood, and time saved for rest and exploration are significant, if underrated, advantages.

Towards a more grounded perspective

School rankings are not going away, nor should they. Used responsibly, they can inform choice and encourage reflection. But they must be read as signals, not verdicts. Education thrives not in rankings but in relationships – between teachers and students, schools and families, learning and life.

As educators, parents, and institutions, the task before us is to look beyond the glitter. To ask deeper questions. To value substance over spectacle. And to remember that the true measure of a school is not in how it ranks, but how well it nurtures confident, curious, and compassionate learners – day after ordinary day.

Anuradha C is an IT industry drop-out after several years of slogging and money-making. She is now working freelance as a corporate technical trainer and content writer. She is hoping to channelize her passion for writing into a satisfying experience for herself and a joyous experience for her readers. She can be reached at anuradhac@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Anuradha C
Do rankings truly reveal an institution’s educational quality? Anuradha C reflects on school rankings and the realities they often miss.

item.title: Crafting rubrics that nurture growth
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Monica Kochar

As educators, we stand at a powerful crossroads. With every interaction, every task we design, we hold the potential to either ignite a student's passion for learning or inadvertently dim their curiosity. When it comes to assessment, what tools we employ become very important. Among these tools, the rubric stands out – a seemingly objective framework, which when wielded with care and empathy can become a beacon of clarity and a catalyst for growth in middle school math classrooms. But, if approached without thoughtful consideration, it risks becoming a rigid cage, stifling the very creativity and individual expression we aim to foster.

What exactly is this instrument we call a rubric? At its core, it's a guide – a set of performance requirements that articulate different levels of excellence for a given task (Brookhart, 2013). Think of it as a detailed map outlining the journey of learning, with clear milestones indicating different levels of mastery. However, a map is only useful if its symbols and routes are clearly explained. Similarly, for a rubric to truly serve our students, the criteria and their descriptions must be transparent and well-understood (Laurian & Fitzgerald, 2013). When crafted and communicated effectively, a rubric transforms from being a mere grading tool into a valuable assistant for both students and teachers, illuminating the path towards deeper understanding and enhanced learning. It broadens the scope of evaluation, allowing us to look beyond just the final answer and appreciate the nuances of the learning process.

Yet, as we embrace the structure that rubrics provide, we must also tread with caution. Drawing upon the principles of constructivism, we recognize that each child's mind is a unique landscape, with its own pathways of understanding and interpretation. Learning is a personal journey, distinct for every student. Therefore, a potential pitfall of using rubrics is the inherent tendency towards uniform evaluation, even if some space for diversity is intentionally built in. Even the most meticulously designed rubric cannot fully capture the individual needs of students, constructive criticism delivered with empathy, and opportunities for genuine engagement (Qasim & Qasim, 2015). There's a risk, however subtle, that students might begin to "work for the rubric," focusing solely on meeting the defined criteria rather than cultivating their own independent thinking and problem-solving approaches (Chapman & Inman, 2009).

The teacher’s role

This is where our artistry as teachers comes into play. Crafting rubric descriptors that are broad enough to allow ample room for learners to maneuver, to explore different avenues of thought and expression, is not merely a technical exercise – it's an act of nurturing young minds. It requires us to step into their shoes, to anticipate their diverse interpretations and approaches. When this delicate balance is not achieved, the application of rubrics can lead to inconsistencies in how students are assessed, potentially creating feelings of unfairness or confusion.

A few examples

From my own experiences in the middle school classroom, I've come to deeply appreciate the power of rubrics when used thoughtfully. When learning outcomes are best demonstrated through tangible performance – a presentation, a project, a problem-solving process – rubrics become invaluable guides (Brookhart, 2013). Here are some ways I've strived to use them with heart and intention:

  • Co-creating the compass: One of the most empowering experiences is to involve students in the creation of the rubric itself. By engaging in a whole-class discussion to define the parameters of assessment, the different levels of achievement, and the reasoning behind them, we foster a sense of ownership and understanding. When students have a hand in building the compass, they are far more likely to use it effectively to navigate their tasks and engage in meaningful self-reflection.
    • Example (Data Representation Project): For a project where students need to represent a given dataset using charts, we can collectively discuss what constitutes an effective chart (clear labels, accurate scales, appropriate chart type) and define different levels of achievement for each criterion.
  • The rubric as the first point of reference: When students approach with questions about specific task details – "Should my graph be coloured?" or "How many examples should I include?" – my gentle redirection is always, "What does the rubric say?" This seemingly simple response encourages them to internalize the rubric as their primary guide, fostering independence and the ability to self-direct their learning. Often, this leads to students proactively saying, "I think I've met these criteria, but I need to work on this section for improvement."
  • Broad strokes, not rigid lines: I consciously avoid creating rubrics that are overly prescriptive, which dictate every minute detail. Instead, I aim for broader descriptors that provide a framework while allowing students the freedom to think creatively and bring their own unique perspectives to the task. This acknowledges that there isn't always one "right" way to demonstrate understanding.
    • Example (Solving Word Problems): Instead of a rubric that specifies each step of the algebraic solution, a broader rubric might focus on "understanding the problem," "choosing an appropriate strategy," "executing the strategy," and "explaining the solution," allowing students to employ different valid methods.
  • Celebrating diverse talents: To honour the multiple intelligences present in our classrooms, I ensure that tasks offer varied avenues for demonstrating understanding, and that the rubric reflects this diversity. A mathematical concept can be explored through a written explanation, a visual representation, a dramatic enactment, or even a musical piece. Furthermore, I differentiate the product by allowing students to present their work in a format that resonates with their strengths and interests. Their individual viewpoints and creative choices are valued and reflected in the assessment (Chan & Ho, 2019).
    • Example (Pythagorean Theorem): A rubric for demonstrating the understanding of the Pythagorean theorem could include criteria for accuracy of calculation, clarity of explanation (written or oral), visual representation of the theorem, or even a creative application of the theorem in a real-world scenario presented through a skit or a model.

Conclusion

Our ultimate aim as teachers is to cultivate life skills in our students. We design rigorous tasks to challenge them and foster their growth. But these tasks are only meaningful when coupled with equally rigorous rubrics – frameworks for evaluation that are comprehensive, flexible, and deeply considerate of the student's thought processes. There is little value in assigning a complex project on the properties of circles and then solely assessing the accuracy of the final calculations.

We must create rubrics that delve deeper, that acknowledge the journey of learning, the critical thinking involved, and the unique ways in which each student arrives at their understanding. Crafting and applying such rubrics takes time and thoughtful effort (Brookhart, 2013), but the reward lies in witnessing our students flourish, not just as test-takers, but as independent, creative, and confident learners.

References

  • Brookhart, S. (2013). How to create and use rubrics for formative assessment and grading (pp. 3-14). ASCD. http://www.ascd.org/Publications/Books/Overview/How-to-Createand-Use-Rubrics-for-Formative-Assessment-and-Grading.aspx
  • Chan, Z., & Ho, S. (2019). Good and bad practices in rubrics: the perspectives of students and educators. Assessment & Evaluation in Higher Education, 44(4), 533-545. https://doi-org.proxy1.lib.trentu.ca/10.1080/02602938.2018.1522528
  • Chapman, V., & Inman, M. (2009). A conundrum: Rubrics or creativity/metacognitive development? Education Digest: Essential Readings Condensed for Quick Review, 75(2), 53-56. https://files.eric.ed.gov/fulltext/EJ849020.pdf
  • Laurian, S., & Fitzgerald, C. (2013). Effects of using rubrics in a university academic level. Procedia – Social and Behavioral Sciences, 76, 431-440. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.sbspro.2013.04.141
  • Qasim, A., & Qasim, Z. (2015). Using rubrics to assess writing: Pros and cons in Pakistani teachers' opinions. Journal of Literature, Languages and Linguistics, 16, 51-57. https://www.researchgate.net/publication/285815750_Using_rubrics_to_access_writing_Pros_and_cons_in_Pakistani_teachers'_opinions

Monica Kochar is a seasoned educator and curriculum designer specializing in math education. With over 10 years of experience, she created HumaneMath, a human-centric math program, and has worked with leading organizations in edtech, publishing, and training. Her expertise spans curriculum design, teacher training, assessments, and quality review. Monica has contributed to key startups, including WhiteHat Jr., and mentored several initiatives. She holds a Master’s in Education from the University of the People. She can be reached at humanemath@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Monica Kochar
Assessments are vital to learning, but can we use these tools with care and empathy? Monica Kochar writes about how we can move beyond rigid grading checklists and design them for deeper learning.

item.title: Reimagining Indian schooling
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Prateek Maheshwari and Aishwarya Poojari

India's school education system, particularly at the primary and middle levels, has long been a subject of concern. While access to education has improved over the last few decades, challenges related to delivering quality, student engagement, and academic outcomes continue to persist. The Annual Status of Education Report (ASER) 2022 by Pratham, highlights a significant decline in foundational learning among Indian schoolchildren. Nationally, only 42.8% of class 5 students could read a class 2-level text in 2022. Similarly, just 25.6% of class 5 students could solve a basic division problem. These findings indicate deep-rooted issues such as poor early-grade instruction, inadequate teacher training, and a curriculum that often prioritizes rote-learning and syllabus coverage over comprehension.

The root of the problem lies in the system's structure (Robinson, 2006). An exam-oriented and syllabus- driven framework reduces learning to a transactional activity. In his famous TED Talk ‘Do Schools Kill Creativity?’, Sir Ken Robinson mentions that students are often passive recipients of information rather than active participants in knowledge construction and learning. This factory-like model of education, designed originally to produce a clerical workforce during the colonial times, has proven increasingly inadequate for the developmental needs of 21st century learners (Robinson, 2015).

Despite decades of academic discourse and a few reforms (RTE Act, 2009; NCF-SE 2023, etc.), the system continues to prioritize rote memorization over conceptual clarity, standardized tests over creative assessment, and compliance over curiosity. The problem is not new – it is structural, deep-rooted, and requires a paradigm shift. In this direction, the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 aims to mark a pivotal shift in India’s approach to school education, attempting to address deep-rooted systemic challenges through a more inclusive, flexible, and learner-centric model. It calls for a departure from rote memorization and content-heavy textbooks, advocating instead for competency-based, hands-on learning that nurtures creativity and critical thinking. The policy places strong emphasis on Early Childhood Care and Education (ECCE), proposes teaching in regional languages to enhance accessibility, and integrates vocational and skill-based education from grade 6 (NEP, 2020).

NEP 2020 also seeks to eliminate the rigid boundaries between academic and co-curricular subjects, encouraging a multidisciplinary style of learning and recommends a redesign of board examinations to better assess students' understanding of core concepts and skills rather than their ability to recall facts. However, its implementation remains a significant challenge till date, due to several reasons. A study reveals significant disparities in NEP readiness across Indian states (CPHRE Policy: Governance and Management of Higher Education (Policy Brief No. 6)). Teacher training continues to be uneven, with large numbers of educators still awaiting proper orientation on child-centric and experiential pedagogies. Infrastructural gaps, especially in rural areas, make foundational reforms difficult. Moreover, the existing assessment and evaluation mechanisms continue to be rooted in traditional testing models, which discourage experimentation.

Furthermore, public awareness of NEP 2020 remains low, especially among key stakeholders like teachers, parents, and students. A survey carried out in Tamil Nadu found that only 34% of the respondents were aware of the major provisions of the NEP (Venkatesan, 2020). Another national-level study assessing awareness among higher education learners and educators revealed that less than 25% had a clear understanding of how NEP 2020 impacts their academic progression, pedagogy, or institutional governance (Dahiya, 2024). These figures reflect a significant disconnect between policy intent and public engagement, making awareness-building a crucial first step in achieving meaningful education reform. Without support and involvement from the community, even the most thoughtfully designed reforms risk remaining on paper, with little or no change in classrooms. While NEP provides a clear direction, turning its vision into meaningful change will require time, consistent investment, and committed efforts across all levels of the education system.

Amidst these challenges, experiential and adventure-based education offers a hopeful path forward and may emerge as a compelling alternative. It addresses systemic gaps by reimagining learning as active, inquiry-driven, and deeply rooted in the learner’s context.

Educators (Chapman et al., 1992; Katula and Threnhauser, 1999; Fenton and Gallant, 2016) define experiential and adventure-based education as educational methodologies that prioritize learning through action, reflection, and real-world engagement. Globally, several countries have adopted/moved toward such models, with impressive results. For instance, in Finland, schools adopt a thematic approach. Subjects are taught in integrated modules, and students engage in collaborative projects rather than textbook-driven learning. Notably, Finland has no national standardized exams until high school. Similarly, New Zealand embeds outdoor and place-based education into the curriculum. Students engage with local ecosystems, indigenous Māori knowledge systems, and community-based projects to learn geography, science, and history. Research showed improved academic performance, student wellbeing, and community engagement in schools that adopted experiential learning pathways (Ministry of Education, New Zealand, 2021).

The United World Colleges (UWC), which have campuses in over 18 countries, combine rigorous academics with experiential components such as social service, nature immersion, and intercultural collaboration. UWC Mahindra College in Maharashtra, for instance, requires all students to participate in outdoor expeditions, community development projects, and sustainability initiatives. Likewise, High Tech High in California (USA) offers a project-based curriculum where students solve real-world problems in partnership with local communities. Studies (Arifin et al., 2019; Hart et al., 2021) have found High Tech High graduates outperforming their peers in college retention and critical thinking assessments. In this context, India has had its share of successful experiments with experiential and adventure-based education, though most remain on the periphery. Schools such as The Riverside School (Ahmedabad), Shikshantar (Udaipur), Auroville Schools (Tamil Nadu), and Rishi Valley School (Andhra Pradesh) have demonstrated that child-led, hands-on learning can lead to deep engagement and creativity.

Despite such initiatives in our country and their proven success, most parents and educators still remain unconvinced. The fear that alternative pedagogies may not adequately prepare students for competitive entrance exams continues to dominate educational choices. As a result, even students who are exposed to experiential models during early years of schooling often return to conventional systems by high school, losing the benefits of a more holistic foundation. This gap becomes even wider because there is no strong system in place to support it. Without proper training for teachers, support from schools, and models that can work at scale, experiential learning is often seen as a luxury – something only elite or alternative schools can afford – instead of a basic need for all students.

One might ask why there is a need for experiential and adventure-based education at the school level. The answer lies in the fact that several research studies (Shonkoff and Phillips, 2000; Heckman, 2006; Lillard et al., 2013) indicate that the early years are a critical period for cognitive, emotional, and social development. The brain’s plasticity peaks between the ages of 0 and 6, making it a uniquely powerful window for learning through experience and interaction. Cognitive functions, emotional regulation, language development, and social learning are most effectively nurtured during this period. Prevailing educational models, however, delay meaningful learning experiences until later years, often after this critical window has passed (Rubin et al., 2009). Moreover, rising levels of student stress, screen addiction, and academic disengagement indicate a failure to address the emotional and psychological needs of learners (Bullard, 2023; Öztekin, 2024).

In an era where Artificial Intelligence (AI) can process and retrieve information more efficiently than humans, the role of education must shift from information delivery to skill development, emotional resilience, critical thinking, and collaboration. Experiential and adventure-based education directly addresses these dimensions by placing the learner at the centre of the learning process. Adapting such approaches to the Indian context will require effort, innovation, and policy support. Adventure-based learning, nature classrooms, storytelling pedagogy, skill-based learning modules, and community- integrated curricula offer viable pathways. But these need to be mainstreamed, not marginalzed. Education reform cannot be the sole responsibility of government agencies. It requires collective investment from educators, researchers, parents, civil society organizations, and private sector actors. Strengthening teachers' capacity, implementing curriculum and assessment reforms, continuous research on experiential learning, creating public awareness and collaborating across sectors should be the immediate next steps to develop an experiential and adventure-based education ecosystem and policy support.

Though NEP 2020 has offered India a crucial opportunity, policies alone cannot create the desired change – it's how they’re implemented that matters the most. Everyone involved in education has a role to play in making sure that education and learning in India are not just fair and available to all, but also truly meaningful and impactful. And…adoption of experiential and adventure-based learning may be a steppingstone in that direction.

Dr. Prateek Maheshwari is a faculty member at the Indian Institute of Foreign Trade (IIFT), GIFT City, Gandhi Nagar, Gujarat, an institute under the Ministry of Commerce and Industry, Government of India. Before this, he was working at IIFT, New Delhi. He can be reached at prateek@iift.edu.

Aishwarya Poojari is the co-founder of Outdoor Classroom, an experiential learning initiative focused on learning beyond classrooms. With over 8 years of experience mentoring K-12 students, she believes in holistic approaches to education. She can be reached at aishwarya.poojari2001@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Prateek Maheshwari and Aishwarya Poojari
In an age of AI, declining learning outcomes, and rising student disengagement, Prateek Maheshwari argues for reimagining Indian schooling through experiential education.

item.title: Each One Reach One: empathy in action
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Nivedita V. Bedadur

“They asked themselves just one question! What if we reach out to just one child each, support just one child at a time. Can we change our world? And thus was born an initiative, a campaign, a journey towards empowerment for children who had been left behind for one reason or other.”[1]

What happens when a teacher mentors a child, builds a relationship, and supports them emotionally? Deputy Commissioner Regional Office, Guwahati Region, Assam, Shri Chandrashekhar Azad, recollects…

“Today a class 12 student who was once suicidal and had said that she felt like giving up in life because she failed in class 9 is sitting on the front bench, eagerly listening to her teacher and participating in class activities. Looking back she tells me how her teacher mentored her through the difficult days. The teacher says, ‘I did not do much, I just held her hand when she needed it.’”

It seems impossible to scale up such a one-on-one mentoring programme for students to an entire region, marked by difficult terrain and diverse microclimates. Yet, the teachers, principals, and even miscellaneous staff of the 42 schools of Kendriya Vidyalayas of the Guwahati Region of Assam made it happen.

Kendriya Vidyalayas are schools established by the Human Resource Ministry to provide uninterrupted education to the children of transferable government employees and defence personnel. The vision of Kendriya Vidyalayas is to foster a spirit of national integration, innovation, and experimentation. The Kendriya Vidyalaya Sangathan is committed to improving the quality of education in line with NEP 2020, through innovative practices in its 25 regions. Each one Reach One is one such inclusive and innovative initiative.

Each hand extended is a life transformed…

It all began when the Deputy Commissioner, Shri Chandrashekhar Azad, spoke to the children of class 12 about their feelings and fears related to studies and examinations. In a shocking revelation, the anonymous chits spoke about anxiety, depression, fear of examination and of certain subjects, and even emotional breakdown. Many children had written about disappointing their parents and teachers. Visibly moved, the Deputy Commissioner appealed to the teachers with an inclusive and innovative plan, Each One Reach One. This was not a policy or an official order, it was simply an appeal from one heart to another. Yet, it was a massive plan, with a strong purpose, to reach out to the 42 schools of the region. The Guwahati Region of Kendriya Vidyalaya Sangathan comprises all the Kendriya Vidyalayas in Assam. A simple appeal, not an official order, and yet teachers, headmasters, principals, Assistant Commissioners, and even the administrative staff responded overwhelmingly. They pledged to mentor and support one child each, who had been identified with emotional, conceptual, or academic issues and challenges. It was a long-term engagement for the entire year of 2024-25. Although each mentor only supported one child, it was an arduous task as it involved home visits and engagement with the child beyond school hours, emotional investment, and a responsibility to bring joy and nurture the child.

KYC: Know Your Child – a simple but profound mantra

Her mentor teacher had almost given up on the mentee, she just did not seem interested in studies. Such a challenge was a common experience. Then, in a mentor meeting, the Deputy Commissioner suggested visiting the child’s home. It was discovered that the parents had not given an address and nothing was known about the child’s home. The child too was reluctant to share it. After many enquiries, through a friend, the teacher visited the child’s home and was shocked to discover that her parents were no more and that she was living with her aunt, who was not very welcoming. Obviously, the child had no home support. Realizing this, the mentor was deeply moved and their interactions became richer. Teachers soon began to see the truth of the Deputy Commissioner's one and only principle of mentoring success, KYC, i.e., Know Your Child. A glance at the common support schedule reveals the depth of thought behind the KYC. It also highlights the coordination of different teachers who support the mentor.

[2]

Children in the Guwahati Region are supported by being heard, recognized, and valued. They are also helped to develop conceptual clarity. As each child with special needs was paired with a mentor, it became evident that much more needs to be done. Plans had to be tailor-made for the academic progress of each child, worksheets and interactive teaching learning materials had to be planned to bridge gaps, wellness and mindfulness activities had to be designed. Self-affirmation activities had to be designed, parents had to be taken into confidence and after-school visits had to be planned. It was a massive task, an onerous responsibility for every member indeed. Yet, this voluntary task was done with a smile and the satisfaction of being the light in a child’s eyes!

“Behind every child in need is a story!”

While the impact of the appeal spread wide and deep, mentors undertook a need analysis of children from Balvatika to grade 12. The need analysis revealed that out of a total strength of 41, 541 students from 42 schools, 2026 students needed help. Amongst them, 12.6% had attention related challenges, 9.01% had emotional challenges, 21.7% students struggled with STEM subjects, and 29.4% faced literacy difficulties. Child and mentor were paired on the basis of voluntary adoption and skill areas. The mentor then did several rounds of mentor-mentee meet and greet to get to know the child. The mentor engaged with the parents and class teacher of the child to understand the child’s challenges. A tentative plan was then made in agreement with the child and parents. Often, the mentor visited the child at home and engaged with them beyond school hours. Careful records were maintained using templates designed by the regional office. Regular meetings were organized and the Deputy Commissioner himself motivated the mentors.

“I had almost given up on myself after failing in two subjects. But my mentor never gave up on me. I started trying again because my mentor believed I could,” says a class 7 student. Most students faced challenges at home – broken families, conflicts, and illness. As mentors shared their experiences with each other, one of them remarked, “It is only after becoming a mentor that I realized that every disturbed or non-performing child has a story to tell, which will touch your heart.”[3]

From challenges to confidence

This mission impacted more than 2000 students out of the 41,541 students in the region, rekindling hope, self-esteem and a love of learning in them. Unlike a large scale one-size-fits-all initiative, this programme engaged the human factor, while demonstrating sustainability and scalability. The impact of the mission reached and included almost five percent of the students with challenges. The regional office of Kendriya Vidyalaya Sangathan at Guwahati organized large scale training for mentors in wellness activities as well as SMART (Specific Measurable Achievable, Relevant, and Timely) tools for measuring the impact of all three domains: psychomotor, cognitive, and emotional. This empowered group of mentors helped their mentees set small goals and celebrate their successes. They showed them the path to bridge their learning gaps, supported them in building communication skills and emotional reassurance. This led to 99.9% pass rate in classes 9 to 12.

Looking back and forward…

This programme completed one year in May 2025. Yet, it is not over, it continues with the same spirit in the Guwahati Region as well as the other regions of Kendriya Vidyalaya Sangathan. The Commissioner of KVS has recommended that all regions should plan to take up this initiative. This is because Each One Reach One is not just a campaign, but a heartfelt commitment to inclusivity, compassion, and community building.

References

  1. (2025) Assam Teachers combat student anxiety and academic stress through ‘Each One Reach One’. KRC Times. krctimes.com.
  2. (2025) A Story of healing and holistic growth, neindiabroadcast.com July 28.
  3. (2025) Beyond the blackboard: Assam Teachers turn mentors to help students tide over anxiety disorders, education.com July 28.
  4. Kendriya Vidyalaya Sangathan dsel.education.gov.in.
  5. (2025) Each One Reach One: The KVS mentoring changing lives education economic times.indiatimes.com.
  6. (2025) Each One Reach One: Transforming students and lives through mentorship.
  7. NEP 2020 Success Stories, Ministry of Education @EduMin of India. X.EduMin Of India.
  8. (2025) One person can change another person’s world. https: //cdnbbsr.s3was.gov.in.

[1] The author would like to acknowledge with gratitude the inputs given in a telephone conversation by the Deputy Commissioner of Guwahati Region in this article. The author also acknowledges inputs from the material released by the regional office of the Kendriya Vidyalaya Sangathan on its social media accounts.

[2] This table has been adapted from the KVS RO Guwahati Region PPT titled Each One Reach One, which is in the public domain.

[3] Adapted from TGT English KV Guwahati Region as quoted in the social media.

Nivedita V. Bedadur is happiest when she is with children and teachers, learning, unlearning, and teaching. Over years of mentoring and leading teacher capacity development, she has refined her listening and relearning muscle. Presently, she is researching for her upcoming book on the lives of the marginalized people of Konkan. She can be reached at nitavbedadur@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nivedita V. Bedadur
Can one teacher change one life? Nivedita V. Bedadur writes about educators across 42 Guwahati schools who proved this was possible.

item.title: Why are Gen Z and Gen Alpha burning out before they start working?
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Shripriya Shankar

“Beta, just finish your engineering degree, get a good job, and then you can do whatever you want.”

Sounds familiar? For many parents, this is the ultimate formula for success. Get into a prestigious college, secure a stable job, and voila! your life is set.

But here’s what’s actually happening.

Many young people today – especially Gen Z and Alpha – are finishing college feeling exhausted, uninspired, and unsure of what they actually want to do. They’re not just tired from their coursework; they’re tired from years of chasing a version of success that might not even be their own.

So, let’s ask ourselves how well we really know our children.

The Indian parent dilemma: when “best for you” isn’t the best for them

I know a young man who completed his BTech, landed a good job via campus placements, and then hesitated. He realized that what he really wanted wasn’t a coding job, but to direct films.

Now, try explaining that to a family that has spent years investing in his engineering degree! The guilt, the anxiety, the disappointment, the “what will people say?” – it’s all very real.

Then, there’s another student, brilliant at academics, who scored exceptionally well in GRE. His path was clear – higher studies abroad, a high-paying job, financial security. But instead, he’s been spending the last year and a half pursuing writing, unable to force himself into a career that doesn’t feel right. His family worries for his future, but for him, taking a job just for the sake of it feels like stepping into a trap.

Now, if you were the parent or teacher, what would you do? Would you push them toward the “safe” path? Or, would you help them figure out one that truly excites them?

The myth of the “perfect path”

We were all raised to believe in a set roadmap:

Study hard → get into a top university → land a good job → earn well → live happily ever after.

But guess what? The world doesn’t work like that anymore. Degrees no longer guarantee jobs.

AI and automation are replacing traditional roles faster than we realize. Creative and passion-driven careers are no longer just side hustles; they’re legitimate, thriving professions.

Mental health is finally being taken seriously, but too many still hesitate to prioritize it over “practical” career choices.

And so, when young people reach the end of this so-called perfect path and realize it’s not what they wanted, they feel stuck. Because no one taught them how to pause, pivot, and explore.

The silent burnout: why so many feel exhausted before even starting

This burnout isn’t about laziness. It’s about emotional exhaustion from constantly living up to expectations.

Signs of pre-career burnout:

  • Feeling lost even before starting a job.
  • Anxiety about choosing the “wrong” career path.
  • Guilt for wanting to step away from traditional career options.
  • Fear of disappointing family and society.
  • A deep need to rest, but feeling like they have to justify it.

By the time many of these kids land their first job, they’re already exhausted. And when they don’t perform at their best, they’re told they “lack passion” or “aren’t working hard enough.”

But is it really about effort, or is it about years of suppressed choices?

Job security and high incomes: do they really offer security anymore?

Let’s talk about what’s often at the heart of this conversation – financial stability.

Parents push their kids into engineering, medicine, and finance because they offer job security and good incomes. Yes, financial stability is important. But in today’s world, is there really such a thing as a “secure” job?

Even traditional careers are changing rapidly. AI, automation, and global job markets are shaking things up. A high salary doesn’t always mean job satisfaction. Many well-paid professionals still leave their careers in search of meaning.

The smartest professionals today focus not on a single job but on multiple income streams and financial literacy.

So, the question remains: Is financial security worth it if it comes at the cost of your child’s wellbeing?

What can parents and teachers do differently?

Instead of asking, “What job will make you successful?” try asking, “What kind of work will make you happy to wake up in the morning?”

Encourage exploration: Let children experiment with different interests. Expose them to music, art, coding, writing, or sports without the pressure of making it their career.

Redefine failure: Instead of punishing or pointing at mistakes, teach them to ask, “What did I learn from this?”

Introduce alternative career paths early on: Let them meet people who’ve built careers in unconventional fields so they understand that success isn’t limited to a 9-to-5 job.

Prioritize mental wellbeing: Schools and homes should focus on emotional intelligence, stress management, and self-care alongside academics.

Teach adaptability: The most valuable skill today isn’t memorizing facts, but learning how to adapt.

Encourage your children to develop resilience by fostering their adaptability and problem-solving skills.

The role of parents, teachers, and adults in embracing change from childhood

This burnout isn’t starting in college; it begins in childhood when kids are made to believe their worth is tied only to academic achievement. As adults, we need to create an environment where children feel safe to explore, fail, and discover their true interests.

It starts with a simple shift. How well do you really know your child?

Are they happy? Do they feel heard? Do they see a future they are excited about? Or are they just following a script written for them by someone else?

What Nature teaches us about growth and balance

Human beings are not separate from Nature – we are deeply interconnected. Yet, somewhere along the way, we have forgotten to look at Nature as a guide.

Think about a tree. A sapling doesn’t force itself to grow overnight. It takes time to root itself deeply, drawing nourishment from the soil, adjusting to the environment, and stretching towards the sun at its own pace. No two trees grow the same way, even if planted at the same time. Some take longer to flourish, some face storms and lose branches before growing stronger.

Children are no different. Just like plants need the right soil, climate, and care to thrive, children need an environment that allows them to explore, fail, adapt, and find their true path. When we try to force every child into the same mould, we strip them of their natural ability to grow in their unique ways.

Nature thrives on diversity, balance, and adaptability. Isn’t that what we should encourage in our children too?

The future belongs to those who dare to choose differently

Burnout before entering the workforce is a real crisis for the entire society – not because this generation is lazy, but because they’re being forced into an outdated definition of success.

As parents, teachers, and mentors, we have a choice. We can continue pushing them down the same path and hope for the best. Or, we can guide them toward a future where success isn’t just about money – but about happiness, fulfilment, and a life they truly love.

So, what’s it going to be…?

Shripriya Shankar is an educator-turned-natural farmer and agripreneur, living and working from her farm in South India. She is the co-founder of Drśhya Farms, a nature-based initiative rooted in well-being and the betterment of small farmers. Her work is shaped by experiences across mainstream and alternative education, including homeschooling. You can find out more about her work at www.drshyafarms.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Shripriya Shankar
Today’s youth are exhausted even before their careers begin. Shripriya Shankar delves into the pre-career burnout that’s plaguing Gen Z and Gen Alpha.

item.title: Celebrating clouds
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Sujata C

Why are clouds so fascinating? Clouds invite imagination even as they obey the strictest laws of physics. They drift between science and poetry, geography and mythology, observation and admiration.

Clouds are not just weather elements; they are storytellers, water carriers, climate indicators, metaphors, and mirrors of human emotion. This project is designed to cut across age groups, allowing younger children to explore through play and older students to investigate through inquiry and reflection.

The project is meant to arouse a sense of curiosity and wonder in students and invites teachers to use clouds as a unifying theme across subjects. It encourages learners to observe closely, think scientifically, express creatively, and understand the cultural context of clouds.

So what are the learning outcomes we can expect from this project? By engaging with this project, students will:

  • Understand the science of cloud formation and types.
  • Observe weather patterns and relate them to geography and climate.
  • Explore cultural, mythological, and literary interpretations of clouds.
  • Use clouds as inspiration for creative writing, poetry, art, and music.
  • Develop skills of observation, classification, recording, and presentation.
  • Connect environmental awareness with climate change and sustainability.

The science of clouds

What are clouds? At a scientific level, clouds are visible collections of tiny water droplets or ice crystals suspended in the atmosphere. They form when warm, moist air rises, cools, and condenses around microscopic particles like dust, pollen, or salt, which, in scientific terms, are called condensation nuclei. This information opens the door to the discussion of multiple scientific concepts like states of matter, heat and temperature, water cycle, air pressure, and atmospheric layers.

Going further you can explore the different types of clouds with your class. Introduce students to cloud classification. Encourage observation rather than rote learning.

Cumulus – Puffy, cotton-like clouds associated with fair weather

Stratus – Flat, grey clouds that blanket the sky, often bringing drizzle

Cirrus – Thin, wispy clouds found high in the sky, made of ice crystals

Nimbus – Rain-bearing clouds

Cumulonimbus – Towering clouds associated with thunderstorms and lightning

Height at which clouds are found

Clouds take on different shapes depending on the height at which they form – low, middle, and high. Experts study the height, shape, and movement of clouds to predict weather conditions. Based on long-term weather patterns, the planet is divided into climatic zones: tropical, dry, temperate, continental, and polar. Cloud formations vary from zone to zone.

Activity:

  • Find out the exact height of low, mid, and high-level clouds.
  • Conduct a rain-in-a-jar experiment in class.

Cloud watching is a popular recreational pastime. The scientific study of clouds is called nephology and a person who loves watching clouds is called a nephophile. The natural human tendency to see patterns and figures in clouds is called pareidolia.

  • Young students will enjoy an activity of decoding cloud shapes.
  • Write a story: If I could ride a cloud…. Is there any fictional character that rides clouds?
  • Form a cloud watcher’s club and exchange notes with members of the club at weekly meetings.

Weather phenomena

Cloudbursts have become a common phenomenon in recent times. Discuss the reasons for this. India’s worst cloudburst occurred in June 2013 at Kedarnath, where over 6000 people lost their lives.

Activity:

  • Prepare cloud classification charts.
  • Keep a weather diary.
  • Do simple experiments on evaporation and condensation.
