  • Document cloudburst events in India and the world in the past two and a half decades. Compare the data with previous decades.
  • Prepare a case study of the Kedarnath cloudburst.
  • Find out how the Doppler radar works to predict long-term weather conditions.

Clouds and geography

Clouds do not behave the same everywhere. The geography of a place shapes the sky. Coastal regions experience different cloud patterns compared to deserts. Mountains create orographic clouds, where air is forced upward. Monsoons depend heavily on cloud movement and moisture content.

  • Meghalaya – the abode of clouds – receives some of the highest rainfall in India. Are there any other places in India or the world with cloud-related names? Find out.
  • Make a list of names for babies related to clouds.
  • Map cloud types seen in different regions of India using weather reports, satellite images, or newspaper clippings. Discuss monsoons, rainfall distribution, agriculture, and water resources.

Bird, animal, insect behaviour, and clouds

Birds detect change in air pressure, temperature, and wind when clouds gather. Migratory birds fly both above and below cloud cover. Different species of birds fly at different altitudes based on weather and wind to optimize their journey. Most birds fly below the cloud cover. Large migratory birds fly above the clouds.

Activity: Watch documentaries to see how birds navigate clouds and write a report.

Goats will huddle as cumulonimbus clouds gather before heavy rain and thunder. Ants shift their eggs to safer places at the first sign of rain, when dark and heavy clouds cover the skies. Dragon flies swarm, peacocks start calling and dancing in the forests when clouds gather. In the puranas there are references to the Chataka bird that calls out when clouds gather because it wants to quench its intense thirst with rain water. This bird is the pied cuckoo in modern terminology.

Activity:

  • Collect more such fascinating information about bird, animal, and insect behaviour linked to clouds and make a presentation.
  • Record your own observations when clouds form in the sky. Cool wind and dark clouds go together. What kind of clouds are seen when hot winds blow? Discuss.

Clouds in culture

Long before satellites and weather apps, clouds were considered messages from the gods that had to be decoded. Clouds in Indian culture are not just water vapour; they are living entities, divine servants, emotional reflections, highlighting a deep ecological and spiritual connection that human beings have with clouds.

In Western culture, dark clouds are ominous and stand for turbulence, whereas in India, Neela Megha Syama (meaning dark as the rain-filled cloud) is used to describe both Lord Rama and Lord Krishna. The colour is considered attractive, symbolic of divinity and infinity and borders on black for Krishna and is closer to the blue of blue lotus petals for Rama. White clouds symbolize peace in Eastern cultures, notably Hinduism, Buddhism, and Tibetan.

In Greek mythology, there are cloud nymphs – fairy-like beings depicting clouds and rain, shown as beautiful women pouring water from pitchers to nourish the Earth. For the Greeks, clouds are divine messengers. Zeus is the Greek God of sky and weather and is the creator of Nephele, a cloud nymph.

Roman mythology links clouds to Jupiter. Indigenous cultures in other places saw clouds as ancestors, spirits, or omens.

Activity:

  • Which Indian God wields the thunderbolt as a weapon?
  • Paint a scene related to clouds from mythology.
  • Read about the myth of Nephele and Ixion, which highlights how clouds can be used for divine deception or to test mortals.

Clouds and festivals: There are several festivals that celebrate clouds and rain, often as part of broader monsoon or harvest festivals, primarily in India and other parts of Asia. These festivals are rooted in agricultural traditions and show gratitude for rain and the prosperity it brings to the land. In North India, Teej is one such festival. Goa has many monsoon related festivals.

Activity:

  • Find out about cloud and monsoon festivals and make a poster presentation.
  • Monsoon tourism is a big attraction for adventure seekers and nature lovers, as most of India is transformed magically during the rains. Find out the various aspects of monsoon tourism and make a Powerpoint presentation.

Clouds in literature and language

Clouds float freely through literature – from Wordsworth’s wandering clouds to Kalidasa’s Meghdoot, from children’s rhymes to modern haiku.

Clouds have always been rich metaphors. They stand for

  • Change and impermanence
  • Freedom and movement
  • Emotions – joy and sadness
  • Distance and longing
  • Fertility and life

Meghdoot by Kalidasa personifies a cloud as a messenger of love. Read the translations available on the internet. An annual two-day Kalidas Festival celebrates the work of the mahakavi with dance-dramas. Find out when and where this festival is held. Choreograph a small dance-drama based on Kalidas’ Meghadoot.

For more reading/watching:

  • Ashaad ka Ek Din is a well-known play based on the life of poet Kalidas. Prasar Bharati has serialized this play – watch this television adaptation.
  • Ritusamhara: a poem of six cantos in Sanskrit on the six seasons of India.
  • Rabindranath Tagore has written numerous poems on clouds in his books Gitanjali, Stray Birds, and Clouds and Waves.

Language activity:

  • Collect phrases and idioms with clouds and make sentences.
  • Write cloud-inspired poems.

Art and observation

Clouds are Nature’s abstract art. Painting clouds is one of the most relaxing activities for painters. Rain clouds make the sky look dramatic, cauliflower shaped clouds look playful, a cloudy horizon is a sign of trouble brewing.

Activities:

  • Cotton cloud painting.
  • Watercolour sky studies focusing on light and shade.
  • Cloud photography project for older students.
  • Cloud collage using recycled materials.
  • Maintain a cloud journal with drawings.

Encourage students to notice:

  • Shapes
  • Layers
  • Light
  • Movement
  • Colour changes during the day

This builds patience, observation, and visual literacy.

Clouds and music: Clouds have a way of tugging at the heart strings, just like music. The power of music is not always invisible, sometimes it is dramatically visible. In Hindustani classical music Raag Megh and Megh malhar have the power to summon rain. Experts say these raagas express the fury of the monsoon, with sounds of thunder and lightning. Composer, musician, and vocalist Tansen created another version of this raag Miya ki Malhar in the 16th century. Similarly, Raag Amrut Varshini from Carnatic music bonds music with rain. Find out who created this raag.

Experiment: On a hot summer day, listen to Raag Megh, Megh Malhar, and Miya ki Malhar for about three hours or so and document the effects on your mood and the weather.

Make notes: Weather influences the music we choose to listen to. Make notes on the kind of music you listen to in different types of weather.

Clouds are a favourite theme of songs in romantic movies of Bollywood. Bollywood uses clouds and rain to express diverse emotions, from joyful abandon to deep romance and sadness, longing, etc. Clouds in popular songs of the West symbolize dreams, escapism, sadness, peace, and spiritual journeys. Do some research and listen to these songs.

Activity: Sing weather songs.

Clouds and mathematics

Clouds can quietly support mathematical skills. Math becomes meaningful when linked to real-life experiences. Cloud covers are measured in oktas.

Activity:

  • Track the number of cloudy vs clear days in different seasons.
  • Create bar graphs and pie charts using weather observations.

Clouds and environmental studies

Clouds play a complex role in climate regulation. They reflect sunlight, trap heat, and influence rainfall patterns. Changes in cloud behaviour indicate climate instability.

Discuss:

  • Climate change and cloud formation.
  • Urban heat islands and altered rainfall.
  • Climatology and meteorology – possible career prospects for students in pursuing the subject after school.

Activities:

  • Weather data analysis.
  • Climate and cloud correlation study.
  • Research on monsoons and cloud systems.

To wrap up the project, explain how clouds work as calendars, blessings, and warnings; how they dissolve boundaries between subjects and show that knowledge is interconnected. This will help students construct multi-dimensional solutions for problems as adults because they understand the world with greater sensitivity.

Sujata C is an independent writer, editor and lifelong learner. She can be reached at sujata117@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sujata C
Observing the clouds can promote experiential learning in science, art, music, and mythology. Sujata C’s project includes activities like cloud classification, weather diaries and poetry to awaken curiosity in learners of all ages.

item.title: The gamification of Indian classrooms: play with a purpose
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neerja Singh

In India, gamification is gently transforming learning experiences for students. The goal isn't to convert classes into video games, but to create a more natural learning experience by weaving in game elements such as points, levels, badges, challenges, and stories into standard courses. Thoughtfully designed, gamification connects curriculum requirements with student motivation, offering valuable support for teachers facing challenging syllabi, diverse ability levels, resource constraints, and exam-related stress.

In simple terms, gamification involves designing activities that allow children to recognize their progress and achievements as they engage with them. Instead of relying on long memorization drills, a teacher might design captivating "math missions," shared reading objectives, or interactive quizzes that feel more like enjoyable activities than mere evaluations. In educational contexts, including those in India, there is a positive correlation between students' motivation, engagement, and learning outcomes and a shift in their learning experience, even when the syllabus stays the same1.

The DIKSHA platform, which stands for "Digital Infrastructure for Knowledge Sharing," has become a vital framework for large-scale interactive learning in India by bringing together open educational resources, quizzes, and progress tracking that align with state curricula. In numerous DIKSHA-enabled schools, educators evaluate students' understanding through QR-coded texts and short on-screen assessments; dashboards help them identify common misconceptions and create targeted remedial plans. Curriculum-linked gamification shows how digital technologies can enhance instructor discretion while maintaining consistent practices across various classroom environments.

Researchers and educators are discovering that gamified approaches engage students more deeply with the material. A descriptive study published in the Indian Journal of Educational Technology found that incorporating gamification in the classroom enhanced students' engagement, retention, and performance, based on data collected in Malaysia and India. Students in government schools located in rural and low-income areas, as highlighted by NGO-led initiatives such as Bal Raksha Bharat, have the opportunity to design their own educational games through adaptive gamified platforms. Consequently, they show notable advancements in comprehension and involvement.

In India's classrooms, educators blend global perspectives with the unique creativity of their students. These case studies2,3,4 compile anonymized data from gamification research carried out in India and globally, linked to school curricula. In an upper primary school in western India, the math teacher reimagined assessments by writing "missions" on the blackboard. The themes are categorized into bronze, silver, and gold levels, and rather than merely providing answers, students receive simple stars for detailing their problem-solving process. Levels, badges, and collaborative challenges have demonstrated their ability to boost participation and enhance idea retention in state and NGO programmes, and this approach mirrors that effectiveness. Research5,6 indicates that gamified structures can enhance visibility and motivation for diverse learners, as seen when quieter students start participating in group discussions and when families notice their children using "level" language at home.

A class 2 teacher in a low-fee private primary school in northern India introduced a "Story Journey Passport" to enhance reading practice beyond the usual methods. Students earn stamps for completing their reading assignments, and the class comes together to celebrate when a student’s passport is fully filled. This set of classroom examples is conceptually derived from a study carried out with a second grade class that utilized a ‘Reading Race Track’. The students in that class observed notable enhancements in their reading habits, increased time spent reading, and improved skills in decoding, fluency, and comprehension after utilizing progress boards and small incentives. Gamified reading routines that incorporate team challenges, badges, and simple leader boards can boost motivation, accuracy, and speed in reading. What matters most are chances for collaboration and contemplation, straightforward routes for student success, and valuable feedback, as demonstrated by these simple initiatives. The essence of gamification lies in intention, rather than in technology.

Digital technologies are integrated into the image wherever feasible; similarly, anonymized composites illustrate system-level practices as detailed in the DIKSHA and EkStep documentation, along with materials from Kendriya Vidyalaya. In smart class periods, teachers at a Kendriya Vidyalaya primary division in a metro city conduct short interactive DIKSHA quizzes, often initiated by QR-coded energized textbooks. Teachers assess student performance on a short test to guide the following day's toy and activity-based assignments, blending on-screen checkpoints with collaborative hands-on work to address any knowledge gaps that may have arisen from the previous day's quiz. This trend indicates that numerous public and KV schools are incorporating DIKSHA modules alongside activity-based learning, ensuring that technology complements rather than replaces teacher-led instruction. Some private schools have embraced a blended model that combines short modules with discussions, allowing students to engage with curriculum-aligned learning apps for brief periods before addressing common mistakes together. This maintains a connection between digital levels, badges, and streaks with the objectives outlined in the week's textbook. On the other hand, standard lessons in Indian schools collaborate with NGOs and EdTech providers, utilizing adaptive platforms linked to the curriculum and focusing on project-based game design.

Incorporating gamification in education could inspire students to think about their learning journey instead of merely concentrating on the immediate tasks. Students tend to engage more in conversations about strategies, like choosing challenging books or revisiting texts for deeper comprehension, when they can see their advancement on a communal visual tracker, as shown by action research on reading systems like the Reading Race Track. Gamified reading programmes that feature leader boards, unlocking story levels, and badges enhance motivation and metacognitive awareness when connected to debate and reflection, as shown by evaluations. Blended and online learning environments enhance this idea by structuring courses as journeys, featuring badges at significant milestones and including teacher-led discussions where students share their progress. This aligns with broader gamification research highlighting the significance of relatedness, competence, and autonomy as key elements that foster self-regulated learning and boost engagement.

The flexibility of gamification is one reason it succeeds in India's diverse classrooms. Reviews and empirical research indicate that gamification is most effective when it aligns with local realities, is responsive to student data, and is closely connected to learning objectives. These factors hold significant importance in large and varied systems such as India's. National platforms like DIKSHA, along with various NGO and state initiatives, demonstrate a scalable implementation of these ideas. Conversely, classroom level composites like "math missions," "Story Journey Passport," and KV smart class routines illustrate how these principles can be integrated into daily practice, regardless of technology use. Gamification evolves from a temporary trend into a valuable resource for engaging, challenging, and equitable learning when educators spearhead the creation of game-like experiences for their students by establishing goals, offering feedback, and connecting virtual accomplishments to real understanding.

References

  1. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10448467/
  2. https://balrakshabharat.org/blog/others/how-gamification-transformed-indias-curriculum-based-learning/
  3. https://www.smilefoundationindia.org/blog/gamification-of-curriculum-content-of-education-in-india/
  4. https://axonpark.com/how-effective-is-gamification-in-education-10-case-studies-and-examples/
  5. https://journals.ncert.gov.in/IJET/article/view/431
  6. https://www.ijraset.com/best-journal/gamified-platform-for-child-rights-education-in-india-enhancing-legal-literacy-and-empowerm%E2%80%A6

Neerja Singh is a generational diversity speaker, author of six generational books, and a generational coach. A TEDx speaker and certified virtual presenter, she assists multinational companies, educational institutions, and the armed forces to bridge generational gaps. An Oxford University Press Resource Person, she is pursuing a Senior Management Executive Program at IIM Trichy. She can be reached at https://www.linkedin.com/in/neerja-singh/.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neerja Singh
How do points, levels, and badges help students learn better? Neerja Singh takes a closer look at gamification across government, NGO, and private schools in India.

item.title: Reservations, representation, and justice
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Prakash Iyer

Ira: I have been thinking about what you said the last time – essentialism of personal identities. Individuals are often essentialized based on their sex, gender, class, caste, or even character. Essentializing is not always wrong. It is important for us to discriminate between people in order to do the right thing. For instance, I expect people to identify me as a woman, so they behave appropriately.

Anubhuti: But this way of thinking can be risky too. In some ways, we expect them to treat us like women, but with the respect they accord to men; this could be abused too when one thinks, “I am/you are a woman, I/you won’t be able to do this job.” We do essentialize people based on their gender or caste albeit for a good cause – to treat them as equals. That is the genesis of the concept of reservation – a form of positive discrimination, or affirmative action as it is sometimes called. It is ironical though to identify a person by their caste or gender, in order to ensure that they are not discriminated against.

Ira: Words like discrimination are understood only in their negative sense, as if discriminating is wrong. It is the motive behind essentializing that is the issue, not essentializing itself. We have to identify based on caste or gender BUT not identify ourselves by that alone – we are more than our caste or gender.

Anubhuti: This was a strong argument against the special provisions based on caste in the Constituent Assembly debates. K.C. Sharma had said, “It does no good to a depressed class man to be told that because he is a depressed class man, therefore, such and such facilities are provided for him; it does create an inferiority complex in the man. It is not always the giving of facilities here and there that helps so much to raise a man up. It is more a matter of psychological make-up. If a man thinks, “I am as good as A, B, C, or D,” then he raises himself up; the moment you say, “You are an inferior being and therefore, such and such a facility is granted to you and we raise you as against the interests of any other member,” he goes down. He does not raise himself.”[i]

Ira: It seems to me that K C Sharma was arguing against essentializing a person from the depressed class. Wonder how this argument was countered in the Assembly?

Anubhuti: The issue is not whether they are backward or depressed, it is whether they have been kept backward through exclusion and marginalization. The Constituent Assembly members knew caste and religious discrimination to be a social fact.

The notion of providing special provisions for some people was initiated in 1932 by the British prime minister Ramsay MacDonald who recommended that there be separate electoral constituencies for the depressed classes and religious minorities like the Sikhs and Muslims. Babasaheb Ambedkar agreed that this was the best way to ensure that the Scheduled Castes were represented adequately, otherwise even if a dalit stood for election against a person of a different caste, it was unlikely that they would win.

But Gandhi strongly rejected this idea, for he believed this would further isolate the harijans (as he called them) from the mainstream Hindu community. Ambedkar thought that that would be for the best. But Gandhi went on a hunger strike against this proposition and Ramsay MacDonald thought it best to let the two leaders come to an agreement on their own. Ambedkar and Gandhi argued about this and arrived at an alternate system – a joint electorate with seats reserved for people from the depressed classes. The number of such seats was far more than the number suggested as separate electorates by the British. This came to be know as ‘The Poona Pact’, which also called for non-discrimination of the depressed classes in public services and urged for efforts towards fair representation of the community in public services. It also contained a provision that proposed earmarking a portion of the State’s educational grant for the depressed classes.[ii]

Ira: But this was for elections not education and employment. Today reservation applies to both doesn’t it?

Anubhuti: Actually, this argument came up again in the Constituent Assembly when the process of appointment of a minister who would manage the special welfare provisions for tribal communities was being discussed. Some members of the Assembly thought that it did not matter if these positions were filled by people from any community. This was strongly opposed by others, especially Rohini Kumar Chaudhari who said, “The general understanding of the tribal people is that by virtue of this proviso, the tribal people or the Scheduled Castes will secure representation in the Cabinet. If it means that this proviso does not necessarily mean that a member of the tribal people or the tribal people, then, I think this clause is a disappointment to them.”[iii]

Dharam Prakash made a very interesting argument in support of reservations for the Scheduled Castes. He said, “Even now it is an undeniable fact that whenever the question of reservation in services arises, the people of any province holding a majority of posts or the person holding any office are led by provincial or individual interests in making appointments. …If he belongs to his community, he is favoured from the communal point of view and if he belongs to his caste, sub-caste or section, he is favoured from that point of view. The officer does not take into consideration the merit of the candidate but only sees whether he can serve his interest. … the great difficulty which forces us to make a demand for reservation is that there is no such generosity and impartiality in our society as a society needs for its welfare nor is there any possibility of its being there in the near future.”[iv]

He further recommended that we should do away with vague terms like “depressed classes” or “backward classes”, which were created by the British. These terms do not identify people clearly since there are depressed or backward classesin all communities. He suggested we retain Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes and ensure that there is a list of those communities in the Constitution.

Ira: So, there are three different, yet related issues at hand – special provision to take care of the welfare of the Scheduled Tribes, reservation of seats in the parliament for the Scheduled Castes, and education and employment of people from the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes. All these are recommended by the Constitution.

The most unpopular reservations are for higher education institutions and employment in government jobs. What does the Constitution say about that?

Anubhuti: The Constitution only says that the State can provide provisions and lists some restrictions – the most important one being the number of reserved seats in education and jobs should not exceed 50% of those available. The Constitution also does not clearly define SC, ST, or OBC – the last being a more confusing category.

H. J. Khandekar was very elaborate in his justification of the need for reservations. He pointed out how people from these castes are not included in schools, nor given jobs even if they are qualified. He recommended the relaxation of qualifications of people from these groups, so that there is a better chance of them getting these jobs.[v]

Lastly, the debate was on whether reservations should be for a specific period of time – 10 years was the recommendation – or indefinitely.

Ira: That caste-based discrimination continues to exist even today does indicate that we cannot put a fixed timeline on this. We see so much of that on social media, including the worst possible violence. So much so that in 1989 the State had to introduce a law for the “Prevention of Atrocities” for SC and ST people.[vi] Reading Section 3 of the law shows the horrible acts of violence that are anticipated.

Anubhuti: True. Dr. Ambedkar summarized the explanation of the constitutional provisions at the end. He said the solution in the Constitution responded to diverse opinions for and against reservation. He said, …that there shall be equality of opportunity for all citizens.… …although theoretically it is good to have the principle that there shall be equality of opportunity, there must, at the same time, be a provision made for the entry of certain communities which have so far been outside the administration. …the Drafting Committee had to produce a formula which would reconcile these … points of view, firstly, that there shall be equality of opportunity, secondly that there shall be reservations in favour of certain communities which have not so far had a ‘proper look-in’ so to say in the administration.[vii]

It is very interesting that Ambedkar’s suggestion mirrors Rawls’s Difference Principle[viii], which is debated even today.

The basic idea behind reservations is adequate representation and a voice, not merely jobs. The point is that some groups and their knowledge and voices are not represented in a system and in the school curriculum. The purpose should be questioning standard norms rather than integrating people into predominant norms and standards. Like the multiculturalists say, the objective should be to include their voices in mainstream culture not integration or reformation.

Ira: The Constituent Assembly seems to have thought about and anticipated ideas, which political theorists are dealing with today.

Anubhuti: Especially since it seems to bring into question formal equality, which is the foundational principle of democracy. In our Constitution, it has been added to meet the need of substantive equality also – in terms of employment, for which one does require education too. The issue of the creamy layer – those who have already obtained jobs and so are not considered backward any more – is dealt with in asking whether promotions should have reservations too. This remains unresolved in principle. When a person gets a job based on reservation, they continue to be victims of prejudice. No wonder then that we find very few persons from these groups in top positions. For instance, the recent chief justice of the supreme court was only the second dalit judge after 75 years of independence.

Ira: It must be quite difficult to teach this in your class. It must be bringing up a lot of tension like controversial issues are bound to.

Anubhuti: Yes. I hold classroom discussions on the logic behind reservations, empirical data, and personal experiences. The last is the toughest to deal with in class and the most useful too since sometimes personal experiences make a point more strongly than logic does. There is more to think about when we discuss reservations. In class, we have to anticipate different ways of thinking much like Ambedkar did in his defence of reservations. Let’s talk about that next time.

[i] https://www.constitutionofindia.net/debates/01-jun-1949/#105852

[ii] https://www.constitutionofindia.net/historical-constitution/poona-pact-1932-b-r-ambedkar-and-m-k-gandhi/

[iii] https://www.constitutionofindia.net/debates/01-jun-1949/#105862

[iv] https://www.constitutionofindia.net/debates/30-nov-1948/#98159

[v] https://www.constitutionofindia.net/debates/30-nov-1948/#98180

[vi] https://www.indiacode.nic.in/bitstream/123456789/15338/1/scheduled_castes_and_the_scheduled_tribes.pdf

[vii] https://www.constitutionofindia.net/debates/30-nov-1948/#98234

[viii] https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/original-position/difference-principle.html

Prakash Iyer teaches Philosophy of Education and Curriculum theory at Azim Premji University, Bengaluru. The author can be reached at prakash.iyer@apu.edu.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Prakash Iyer
Ira and Anubhuti debate caste, equality, and affirmative action in the Constituent Assembly. Prakash Iyer writes about what discrimination really means, and why the Constitution endorsed special provisions.

item.title: Nurturing environment consciousness
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Pratima Pai

When we moved to North Bengaluru, we chose this area because it was quieter and still in the process of development. The bustling city traffic was a distant thought, and it provided me with an opportunity to cultivate my love for gardening. The calm surroundings, with fewer vehicles on the roads, were a perfect escape from the notorious Bengaluru city traffic.

One day, while returning home from work, I noticed a honeycomb in my neighbour's balcony. The bees, incredibly efficient in their work, inspired me to finally put into practice the idea of gardening in my own space. I decided to explore it further and with some guidance from my friend, started composting. The compost manure we made was soon put to use, and we began growing a variety of vegetables and flowering plants on my balcony. Thanks to the cooler temperatures in this area, compared to the city, I even managed to grow strawberries – something I hadn’t imagined was possible in an urban setting.

As I observed the rapid changes around me, a thought began to take shape. Today, environmental conservation is a pressing concern, yet only a handful of people seem to prioritize it. But shouldn’t it be everyone's responsibility? We all use the environment and its degradation affects us all. This realization led me to the idea that we need to start raising awareness, particularly among students, about the importance of preserving the environment.

There are numerous ways in which we can get students involved. Composting is a simple yet effective practice that they can start at home. They can be encouraged to segregate waste and explore innovative ways to repurpose it. Participating in tree plantation drives is another way for them to directly contribute to environmental conservation. Additionally, they can make seed balls and scatter them in various locations before the monsoon, promoting the growth of natural vegetation.

It is vital to engage students in such activities. They need to understand that the environment they inherit is the one we help shape today. Moreover, incorporating environmental education into school curricula is essential. Instead of focusing predominantly on the pollution and problems caused by environmental degradation, we should emphasize how to conserve and restore the environment. The damage has already been done, and now, the focus must be on practical solutions and actions individuals can take at their level.

Another key aspect is to foster a spirit of volunteerism among students for conservation programmes. When students realize that even small individual efforts can collectively make a significant difference, they will be more inclined to contribute. Volunteering for environmental causes instils a sense of responsibility, and giving back to Nature can be incredibly fulfilling.

Environmental conservation needs to be a collective effort, and students have a pivotal role to play. By involving them in hands-on activities like composting, waste segregation, tree planting, and volunteer programs, we can equip them with the knowledge and motivation needed to protect our planet. After all, the future of the environment lies in their hands.


Pratima Pai is a teacher in a private school who loves engaging in creative work. She enjoys spending time with inquisitive children who keep her on her toes and inspire her to learn something new every day. She is able to turn complex concepts into fun, engaging experiences for her students. Children thrive in joyful classrooms, where curiosity is sparked and growth happens. She can be reached at pratimakishore.pai29@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Pratima Pai
Through simple actions like composting and gardening, we can inspire a lifelong commitment to protecting the environment in children. Pratima Pai makes the case for involving students in environmental conservation efforts.

item.title: A different and delightful take on history
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Priyanka Soman

History, as we know it, often unfolds with kings, queens, battles, and treaties. But in Travelling Treasures, Mala Kumar shifts the spotlight to a rather different cast of characters – your bicycle, umbrella, chewing gum, and even that trusty ketchup bottle that refuses to let go of its contents when you need it the most.

Across a hundred short, sparkling tales, Kumar traces how these everyday objects – some modest, some marvellous – made their journey to India. From the humble pencil that fuels every schoolchild’s imagination to the shiny elevator that whisks you to the mall’s top floor, she reveals the surprising paths they’ve taken, zigzagging across time and continents before finally landing in our lives.

This isn’t the sort of history that sends you searching for caffeine. It’s lively, witty, and full of delightful detours. Kumar has a knack for turning facts into adventures. Traders, rulers, inventors, explorers – and the occasional “creative transporter” (they were simply enthusiastic importers) – all make cameo appearances in this grand global relay race.

Each object becomes a storyteller in its own right. You can almost imagine the umbrella recounting its rainy-day exploits or the bicycle ringing its bell with pride at having outlasted empires. The writing strikes a perfect balance between being informative and thoroughly entertaining – ideal for young readers, history buffs, or anyone who’s ever wondered how the world’s odds and ends found their way here.

The structure – an A-to-Z expedition – adds a sense of playful curiosity. You never quite know what the next page will unveil: a fruit, a fabric, or a fascinating piece of forgotten trivia. And the best part? You can dip in anywhere and still come away feeling a little wiser – and a lot more amused.

By the end of the book, you’ll find yourself looking at your surroundings differently. Your morning cup of tea, or even that safety pin on your desk, suddenly seems like a globetrotter with tales to tell.

Travelling Treasures makes history deliciously human. It invites you to celebrate curiosity, invention, and the extraordinary journeys of ordinary things. Funny, fact-filled, and endlessly fascinating – it’s the sort of book you’ll want to keep handy, if only to remind yourself that even the simplest objects can have the most adventurous pasts.

Rating: 4.9/5 – A joyous, intelligent romp through history. Read it, gift it, and never look at your ketchup bottle the same way again.

Priyanka Soman is an educator and writer with a keen interest in integrating the arts, sciences, and storytelling to create meaningful learning experiences. She can be reached at cspriyankaa@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Priyanka Soman
Would you believe that umbrellas, bicycles, and ketchup bottles sometimes travel through space and time to reach us? Priyanka Soman reviews one such book that delves into the surprising histories of everyday objects.

item.title: Building a scientific Karnataka: one teacher at a time
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Karuna K Simha and Manaswini Vijayakumar

Every teacher steps into a classroom with hope: to make a difference, to light a spark, to shape the future. Between this aspiration and everyday classroom realities, something is lost in translation. Chalk-and-talk methods continue to predominate and syllabi dictate the pace and scope of learning. Learning, in turn, is primarily restricted to memorization, which, if done well, yields results. Despite their best intentions, many teachers are forced into a system that prioritizes exam results over exploration and routine over innovation. But a quiet revolution is underway – one that hands back the joy of discovery to students and the power to transform learning to teachers.

At the forefront of this quiet shift is Prayoga’s Teacher Empowerment Programme (TEP), part of a five-year organizational initiative, Kriya, that reimagines how science is taught in schools. Kriya adopts experiential learning as its core pedagogical approach, encouraging children in their formative years to actively discover, experiment with, and understand scientific phenomena.

Why teachers, why now?

The constraints under which Indian classrooms labour are hidden in plain sight. The compulsion of syllabus completion, unimaginative standardization of assessment and examination, and the emphasis on consistently high scholastic results, together stifle innovation. Teachers, though well-intentioned, quickly settle into familiar patterns that require minimal preparation and ensure predictable outcomes. And yet, despite their reservations, the same teachers respond with enthusiasm when asked if they want their students to improve. Their hesitation toward change is perhaps born of the daunting idea of a system-wide change.

It is at this confluence of systemic change and educator vision that Prayoga steps in.

Recognizing that teachers are the frontline agents of educational change, TEP equips them to move from being instructors to facilitators of learning. In essence, TEP mobilizes a transformation from monologue to dialogue, from delivery to discovery.

Science as a gateway

Science is not just a subject – it is a way of thinking. With fewer students opting for science at the undergraduate level (only 14.7% according to AISHE 2024), the shortage of both science professionals and passionate science teachers poses a serious concern.

Children are naturally curious. Science gives us the perfect opportunity to nurture and promote this curiosity through concrete, hands-on learning experiences.

This innate inquisitiveness is the fundamental motivation that propels Prayoga’s initiative to enable experiential learning of science. This pedagogical approach allows children to understand scientific concepts at a deeper level by keeping alive their propensity for exploration and discovery.

From activity to experience

While many schools conduct activities or experiments, these often remain superficial without the right kind of guidance. A child may pour chemicals or build a model, but without the requisite cognitive scaffolding, activities, no matter how hands-on, will not translate into real learning. Observation, thoughtful questioning, guided reflection, and linking to prior knowledge are all facets of true learning.

Prayoga’s model ensures that every activity becomes a learning experience, led by trained teachers who know how to ask the right questions and create space for student thinking.

The five-year path to teacher transformation

The Teacher Empowerment Programme is a structured, evolving journey that builds teacher capacities year-on-year:

  • Year 1: Teachers begin as Experiential Learning Educators understanding the foundations of experiential learning pedagogy.
  • Year 2: They become Specialists, applying strategies more deeply in the classroom.
  • Year 3: As Pedagogical Designers, they begin to innovate and adapt methods to suit diverse learners.
  • Year 4: Teachers reflect on their growth as Practitioners, refining their practice through structured reflection.
  • Year 5: Finally, they take on the role of Teacher Researchers, who can study, experiment, and improve teaching methods by themselves.

Every stage of the teacher's and learner's progression is equilibrated. Each stage of the progression is done in such a meticulous way that the growth is both tangible and enjoyable. The rigor of this journey is the distinct feature of the program.

This progression is supported by trained academic coordinators who mentor teachers daily, helping them plan lessons, reflect on outcomes, and overcome challenges. The programme envisions the teacher to be independent and capable of undertaking research for their academic and classroom problems.

Transformation –not just training

What makes Prayoga’s approach different is its inherent flexibility to cater to varying classroom realities rather than proposing a one-size-fits-all approach. The programme is rooted in real challenges faced by teachers, beyond the confines of abstract theories. The workshops are grade, content, and context specific, ensuring relevance and immediate applicability.

Science content, pedagogy, and classroom practice are all integrated through what is termed Pedagogical Content Knowledge (PCK). PCK equips teachers with a robust content knowledge of science and the proficiency to teach the subject effectively.

Change unfolds in its own time. Considering their hectic schedules and multiple duties (including administrative), teachers require periodic orientation and revision of experiential learning. While a one-day workshop can spark curiosity and introduce new approaches, it is sustained, on-ground support that truly empowers teachers and ensures continued implementation in letter and spirit. The TEP program provides for continuous engagement through a range of initiatives from online expert interactions and pedagogical videos to classroom demonstration clips, curated WhatsApp messages, and insightful write-ups. The practice of experiential learning and the spirit of innovation are thus infused in the daily classroom.

A timely shift for a fast-paced world

We no longer live in a world where knowledge in itself is sufficient. Today’s students need to be thinkers, problem-solvers, and collaborators. In a world driven by AI and rapid innovation, skillsandcompetencies – notjustfacts – arethecurrencyofsuccess.

Our classrooms, therefore, must change. This change begins with teachers. Prayoga’s TEP offers not just hope, but a plan – for science, for students, and the future of Indian education. If transformation is the goal, this is what its first stirring looks like.

Teacher transformation

At Prayoga, we believe that teacher transformation happens through better support, not through increased pressure. The Teacher Empowerment Programme has consistently shown that meaningful support, encouragement, and relevant tools can make a deep impact on teacher confidence and practice.

This transformation is not about replacing teachers’ existing experience, but about reinforcing it with renewed purpose. Many teachers reflected on how theoretical knowledge from their B.Ed. programs found meaning only after engaging with TEP.

This transformation is not limited to teachers alone. It extends to students, who have grown more engaged and curious.

“I conduct three science experiment sessions a week. If I miss even one, my students ask, ‘Madam, don’t we have a science class today? Won’t we use the materials?’ Their enthusiasm motivates me to make learning even more exciting.”

The impact of this transformation is also spreading beyond individual schools. Many Kriya teachers are invited as resources persons at the cluster and block level. At a recent Cluster Knowledge Sharing Meeting in Kumta district, a Kriya science teacher demonstrated hands-on activities to over a dozen science teachers from the Kagal cluster – exemplifying how empowered teachers further strengthen others.

“The students are so attached to the Kriya classes that they refuse to take transfer certificates,” shared a teacher from a government higher primary school.

“Even after completing higher primary, they are reluctant to move to secondary schools. We had to personally accompany them and recommend a nearby school where the Kriya approach is followed at the high school level.”

Through the teachers’ renewed vigour and vision, a deep sense of connection and continuity in learning has been fostered. There is a significant and palpable cultural shift in student attitudes and approach. These changes are a harbinger of a growing reserve of trust and investment in the education process, fostered as a consequence of Kriya, where both students and parents are beginning to see school as a place of joyful, meaningful, and enduring learning.

Manaswini Vijaayakumar is a public policy graduate from Azim Premji University. She is a research communications professional with research interests across education, law, and health. She is also an education research associate at Prayoga, focussing on policy-related aspects of the domain. She can be reached at manaswini.v@prayoga.org.in.

Dr. Karuna K Simha is an education researcher and teacher educator specializing in reflective teaching, experiential learning, and educational psychology, with advanced expertise in educational statistics, assessment and evaluation tool development, and pedagogical design for experiential classrooms. She has led large-scale longitudinal projects at Prayoga on experiential pedagogy and teacher empowerment, developed standardized tools to assess learner competencies and teacher cognition, and published research on reflective practice and experiential learning in reputed journals. She has also contributed to policy as a member of Karnataka’s NEP 2020 Science position paper and state curriculum framework committees. She has been associated as a resource person with NCERT and DSERT-Karnataka, leading research studies for the state. She has participated in national conferences and conducted various faculty development programmes for in-service teachers. She can be reached at karunaks@prayoga.org.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Karuna K Simha and Manaswini Vijayakumar
How might science classrooms move beyond memorization to experiential learning? Karuna K Simha and Manaswini Vijayakumar write about a programme doing just that.

item.title: The role of Social Constructivist Theory in enhancing linguistic and cognitive skills
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Niraj Chaudhary

Social Constructivist Theory developed by Lev Vygotsky, has had a profound impact on modern language teaching methodologies, particularly in English language classrooms. Its emphasis on the social nature of learning and the active construction of knowledge through interaction makes it especially valuable in the context of English as a Second Language (ESL) education. The theory provides a framework through which both linguistic and cognitive skills can be effectively developed, resulting in deeper learning and greater student engagement. The integration of Vygotsky’s theory into ESL instruction will not only support language acquisition but also foster a more inclusive and equitable educational environment, where all learners can succeed.

Mediation, Internalization, Zone of Proximal Development (ZPD), and Scaffolding are the four major theoretical constructs of Social Constructivist Theory. Mediation refers to the use of cultural tools by learners to solve problems and develop higher-order mental functions. Among these tools, language is the most significant (Chaudhary, 2024). According to Vygotsky, language plays a crucial role in human cognitive development by enabling psychological processes to become semantically mediated. This capability distinguishes human thought from animal cognition. In particular, the development of scientific concepts relies heavily on systematic mediation, analysis, and critical thinking. Internalization is another core principle of social constructivism. Vygotsky proposed that every function in a child’s cultural development appears twice: first at the social level (inter-psychological), and later the individual level (intra-psychological). This progression reflects the transition of knowledge from shared social interactions to internal cognitive processes within the learner's mind. ZPD is a key concept in Vygotsky’s theory and represents the gap between what a learner can do independently and what they can achieve with appropriate support. It highlights the dynamic tension between a learner’s current abilities and their developmental potential. ZPD provides a metaphorical space where participatory and socially mediated learning takes place. It is within this zone that pedagogy and shared understanding (inter-subjectivity) emerge, as described by John-Steiner and Mahn (1996), and cited by Daniels (2001, p. 56). Scaffolding is a vital construct in Social Constructivist Theory, referring to the structured, temporary support provided by teachers or peers to help learners progress toward greater competence. This support allows learners to expand their current understanding and skills by performing tasks they could not complete alone. As learners gain confidence and capability, the support is gradually withdrawn, leading to greater independence and mastery of new concepts.

Social Constructivist Theoretical constructs/ principles can be effectively applied in classrooms to create collaborative learning environments. These environments often involve group work, pair work, discussions, meaning-making activities, project work, and debates. Such strategies enable learners to engage in meaningful interactions, which significantly enhance their language abilities.

Listening: Teachers can support the development of listening skills through group discussions, storytelling, and interactive activities. For example, during group work sessions, learners listen to each other's ideas, respond to questions, and engage in dialogue. These experiences help improve comprehension and active listening skills.

Speaking: To promote speaking, teachers can use pair work, group discussions, and role-playing exercises. In these interactive settings, learners are encouraged to express their thoughts, negotiate meaning, and practice pronunciation. These activities help develop fluency and confidence in spoken communication.

Reading: Collaborative reading activities allow learners to explore texts together and share their interpretations. Techniques such as reading circles and group text analysis can be used to enhance reading comprehension. This approach not only deepens understanding but also allows learners to learn from each other’s perspectives.

Writing: Teachers may assign group writing projects where learners collaboratively brainstorm ideas, draft content, and provide feedback on each other’s work. This process fosters a supportive learning environment in which learners can improve their writing through teamwork and constructive feedback.

Grammar and vocabulary: Interactive activities like sentence construction games, group vocabulary challenges, and role-play exercises help learners apply grammar rules and vocabulary in meaningful contexts. These engaging methods make language learning more dynamic and effective.

Learner autonomy and reflective learning: The concept of ZPD is central to Social Constructivist Theory. It guides teachers and more knowledgeable peers in offering appropriate support based on learners' current levels of understanding. Through targeted assistance – such as prompts, clues, and encouragement, learners can complete tasks they might find too difficult on their own. Over time, this support leads to greater independence. Scaffolding is another essential element of social constructivism. It helps promote learner autonomy by gradually transferring responsibility from teacher to student. As learners receive tailored support, they progress at their own pace, make informed decisions, and take ownership of their learning journey through past experiences and reflections.

Collaboration and critical thinking: Collaborative activities like pair work, group work, and discussions are vital for language development and problem-solving. These tasks enable learners to connect prior knowledge with new content, reflect on their experiences, identify and solve problems, and think critically and creatively. Through collaboration, learners gain a deeper understanding of the material and enhance their ability to analyze and apply what they’ve learned.

In a social constructivist classroom, the teacher takes on the role of a facilitator and guide, encouraging learners to think critically and solve problems collaboratively. For example, during a poetry reading activity, the teacher might divide students into groups, assigning each group specific questions to discuss. These questions could include:

  • What does the poet convey in the first stanza?
  • What expressions or views are presented in the second stanza?
  • What message is the poet trying to communicate in the third stanza?
  • How can you connect the images in the poem to the poet’s ideas?
  • How can we relate this poem to our own lives?

Through group discussions, learners collaboratively explore the meaning of the poem, eventually identifying its main theme. This approach not only deepens their understanding of the poem but also strengthens their ability to analyze literature and connect it to personal experiences.

Conclusion

Social Constructivist Theory has become a highly influential approach in contemporary ESL classrooms. Its focus on collaboration, interaction, and learner-centered instruction aligns well with the needs of language learners. In ESL settings, this approach fosters an environment where students can engage in meaningful dialogue, work together, and build upon each other’s ideas. As a result, learners improve their language proficiency, critical thinking abilities, and essential 21st century skills. Ultimately, this method supports both individual growth and equitable opportunities for linguistic and social development.

References

  • Chaudhary, N. (2024). Constructivist Pedagogy and ESL learning in Nepal. Journal for research scholars and professionals of English Language Teaching. 8 (43), 1-6
  • Daniels, H. (2001). Vygotsky and Pedagogy. Psychology Press
  • Palinscar, A. S. (1998). Social Constructivist Perspectives on Teaching and Learning. Annual Reviews Psychology. 49.345-375

Dr. Niraj Chaudhary holds a Ph.D. in Education from CIE, University of Delhi, MA in Linguistics, University of Delhi, and M.Ed. in English Education from Central Campus, Kirtipur, Tribhuvan University. Currently, he is a faculty member at Hari Khetan Multiple Campus, Burgunj and Director of Research at Axis Pvt. Ltd. Kalaiya. He can be reached at bnp1chaudhary@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Niraj Chaudhary
From theory to classroom practice, Niraj Chaudhary writes about how Social Constructivist principles enhance listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills in ESL learners.

item.title: How do children learn?
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Nidhi Solanki

“Ye tumhare bas ka nahi hai” (you are not capable of doing it)

“Gadhe ho tum” (you are a fool)

“Iske paas dimaag nahi hai” (s/he has no brains)

Many of us have grown up hearing such phrases. For a long time, it was believed, and I wouldn’t be surprised if it still is, that if children don’t score well in exams, it must be because of one of the above reasons.

Statements like these, if repeated often, can deeply harm a child's self-esteem. Modern research, such as James Heckman's work on early childhood development, echoes this by emphasizing how crucial early experiences and environments are in shaping a child's lifelong learning path.

But what is the truth behind such beliefs?

Is it true that a child who takes more time to learn is less smart than the one who learns quickly?

What do we even mean by being "smart"?

Or is it possible that a child isn’t learning because the environment is not conducive?

If that is the case, doesn’t it mean that we need to expose our children to different experiences early-on to help them learn better?

Over the centuries, thinkers and psychologists have tried to uncover the truth behind these assumptions. Without careful investigation, we may end up with a very wrong understanding of children’s learning and development. I recall my first interview at a school, where I was asked, “How do children learn?” I vividly remember replying, "Children are like blank slates," and how quickly the interviewers corrected me! Over the past 12 years of teaching in various schools, many of my earlier assumptions were proven wrong, while some beliefs I had held since my school days turned out to be true. One of the things I have learned is that children bring a wealth of knowledge with them even before they enter school. Their learning can only happen meaningfully when they are engaged and actively involved in the process.

What do we mean by learning?

Learning does not happen in isolation. It occurs through interaction with one’s surroundings. For instance, a child who begins to produce incoherent sounds within a few months of being born turns these sounds into words, and eventually into proper speech by learning from her environment.

Over the course of many years, different psychologists have tried to explore how learning happens and have developed various theories. There are mainly two schools of thought: one is behaviorism and the other is constructivism.

Behaviorism

Behaviorists such as John B. Watson, B.F. Skinner, and Ivan Pavlov believed that learning is a change in behaviour caused by external stimuli such as reward and punishment. This means that if the same stimuli are given, or if the same external environment is provided, different learners can learn the same thing. Here, the role of the teacher is that of an authority figure who controls and directs learning. This view positioned learners as passive recipients of information, while teachers were seen as the givers of knowledge. Teaching methods mostly involved repetition, drills, and practice and reinforcement such as praise and grades. Here, the focus is on the final results, which are assessed through frequent tests.

We see this kind of belief turning into an attitude that creates an environment of fear for children, reinforcing the idea that if they are not doing well, it must be because they are not putting in enough effort. I remember sitting with my books for hours, memorizing and repeating answers, only to end up failing the exams. Clearly, this approach did not help me.

However, as educators and researchers observed children more closely, a new understanding emerged.

Constructivism

Thinkers/educators/psychologists such as Jean Piaget, Lev Vygotsky, and John Dewey believed in a different method of learning, where children actively construct their own understanding and knowledge of the world through experiences and reflection.

There are other types of development, such as emotional, physical, moral, social, and language development. Cognitive development is about how individuals learn to think, reason, and extract meaning from their experiences. Learning involves thinking, reasoning, and making sense of the world. Constructivists like Jean Piaget argued that children are not empty vessels ready to be filled with knowledge from outside; rather, they actively construct their own knowledge.

Let us look at a few constructivist theories in brief:

Piaget’s Theory of Cognitive Development

Jean Piaget studied how children develop the ability to think, reason, and understand the world. He believed that children are naturally motivated to learn and are active learners who construct knowledge through their experiences, rather than passively receiving information.

According to Piaget, learning happens through two key processes:

Assimilation: fitting new experiences into existing ideas.

Accommodation: changing existing ideas or forming new ones when experiences do not fit. For instance, when a child first sees a car, she forms an idea of what a “car” is. Later, when she sees another car, she easily calls it a car, this is assimilation. However, when she sees a large vehicle like a bus and calls it a car, she has to change her understanding and create a new idea for a “bus.” This change in thinking is accommodation. Moving between confusion and understanding helps the child learn.

Piaget proposed that all children pass through four universal and sequential stages of cognitive development, namely Sensorimotor stage (0-2 years), Pre-operational stage (2-7 years), Concrete operational stage (7-11 years), and Formal operational stage (12 years and above). These stages describe how children’s thinking evolves from simple sensory experiences to complex, abstract reasoning, highlighting that cognitive growth is a gradual and active process.

Vygotsky’s Sociocultural Theory of Cognitive Development

While Piaget emphasized the internal cognitive processes of each child, Vygotsky highlighted the powerful influence of social and cultural contexts. According to Vygotsky’s sociocultural theory, children learn through interaction with a "More Knowledgeable Other" (MKO) – this could be a parent, teacher, or peer.

How does a child learn from the environment? Vygotsky proposed that this happens through a process called mediation, which connects the stimulus to the response of the child. A mediator or tool helps in the process of learning. For example, if we want to help a child learn to read a particular word and we place an arrow pointing to that word on a chart, the arrow acts as a mediator. It helps by drawing the child’s attention to the word.

Mediators can draw attention, enhance memory, aid in building better social relationships, and support more effective thinking and understanding. Initially, learners use external mediators (like arrows, visual aids, or checklists). Gradually, they move to using internal mediators, such as mental maps, self-talk, or memory strategies like mnemonics.

Role of the teacher/facilitator

The teacher plays a crucial role in introducing and using external mediators to support children's learning. Once these tools are internalized, the teacher’s role shifts from active guide to facilitator of independent learning. This shift is intentional. The teacher gradually reduces support, allowing the child to take increasing responsibility for their own learning. This gradual release of assistance is what Vygotsky described as scaffolding – a process rooted in intersubjectivity and relationships between the learner and the MKO.

Scaffolding refers to the temporary support provided by the MKO to help the child perform tasks within their ZPD. As the child gains competence, this support is gradually reduced and eventually removed, encouraging independence.

The region between what the child can do on their own and what they can achieve with support from the MKO is called the Zone of Proximal Development (ZPD). ZPD helps us identify the next skill or concept the child needs support in.

Inside the classroom

Despite these rich theories, there seems to be a gap between theory and practice. Inside many classrooms, it is still assumed that children come to receive knowledge while teachers come to deliver it. We continue to ignore the knowledge children bring with them. I wonder why we don’t take into account the backgrounds, cultures, and knowledge that children carry with them. Wouldn’t doing so make learning more meaningful?

Much of education still remains "factory-based", where, as Paulo Freire said, children are seen as passive vessels to be filled with information. Yet, we know from Piaget and Vygotsky that learning thrives when children actively engage with material and when their prior knowledge is respected and built upon. No matter how excellent the teaching or resources, without active participation, real learning cannot occur.

This raises an important question: How can we apply these theories practically in classrooms? To explore this, let's look at two examples: one from an Indian context and the other from a school in Birmingham. Principles from both Piaget’s constructivism and Vygotsky’s sociocultural theory are used in their classroom practices.

Eklavya Foundation: Take a look at this video. In this example, fractions are being discussed in the classroom. We can observe the following from the video:

  • Connecting to context:
     The facilitator connects learning to the child's familiar world – rotis – making the math problem meaningful. Both Vygotsky and Piaget would agree; familiar contexts help anchor new learning.
  • Process over answers:
     Children are encouraged to explain their thought processes instead of being judged solely on correct answers.
  • Time and space:
     Learners are given the freedom to think, write, and collaborate without pressure – essential for cognitive development as Piaget suggests.
  • Encouragement of multiple solutions:
     Different strategies are welcomed, allowing diverse cognitive styles to emerge, resonating with Vygotsky’s emphasis on learning through dialogue.
  • Respect for mistakes:
     Mistakes are seen as part of learning, not something to be punished – a key idea in Piaget's theory of cognitive growth.
  • Collaborative learning:
     Children solve problems together, building understanding through peer dialogue – a clear application of Vygotsky’s principles.
  • Role of the facilitator:
     The teacher acts as a facilitator, gently guiding rather than commanding – a balanced approach that honours both Piaget and Vygotsky.

Birmingham School: In this video, learners are learning how to divide 62 by 5 and we can observe the following points during the classroom learning

  • Individual thinking first:
     Children first attempt problems individually or in small groups, encouraging independent construction of knowledge.
  • Emphasis on "What did you do?":
     Teachers focus on children's thinking rather than simply correcting them, deepening cognitive engagement.
  • Peer learning:
     Peers question and explain to one another, helping children construct and reconstruct understanding socially – Vygotsky’s MKO in action.
  • Large group discussion:
     Different approaches are shared without shaming mistakes, reinforcing both the value of social learning and individual sense-making.

Conclusion

Understanding how children learn is not just a question of teaching strategies, it is a window into how we view childhood itself. Moving away from narrow, deficit-based thinking (such as calling children “slow” or “foolish”) requires us to reimagine learning as a dynamic, social, and deeply personal process. As seen through both Piaget’s and Vygotsky’s theories and reflected in classroom practices such as those at the Eklavya Foundation and the Birmingham school, children are active participants in constructing knowledge, drawing from their innate abilities, experiences, and social contexts. Whether it is a baby grasping objects or a teenager solving abstract problems, every stage of learning involves meaning making, reflection, and support from the environment.

But these approaches are sometimes misunderstood as suggesting that learning is effortless or that facilitators alone are responsible for making learning happen. In reality, learning involves the learner at its core, and without the learner’s active engagement, learning cannot truly take place. Learning demands serious engagement, effort, persistence, and willingness to think, question, and struggle.

What educators and parents can do is commit to creating nurturing spaces where children feel safe to explore, discuss, make mistakes, and design spaces that respect developmental pace while offering appropriate support. At its heart, learning is not about how fast or slow a child moves, but about how meaningfully they are able to connect with ideas, with others, and with the world around them.

References

  1. The Heckman Curve
  2. James Heckman: The economics of inequality and childhood education
  3. A teacher using Piaget’s theory: Constance Kamii
  4. Use of theories: Eklavya Foundation
  5. Kamala Mukunda’s book “What did you learn at school today?”
  6. https://saspublishers.com/media/articles/SJAMS_89_2152-2157.pdf

Nidhi Solanki has worked in different alternative spaces for 11 years, such as Rajghat Besant School, KFI, Anand Niketan Democratic School, and Prakriti School, Noida. She is currently exploring Psychodynamic Psychotherapy and is fond of nature, beaches, and travelling. She can be reached at solankinidhi34@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nidhi Solanki
Why do some children struggle in school despite effort? Nidhi Solanki revisits learning theories from Piaget and Vygotsky to rethink intelligence, pace, and potential.

item.title: Teaching differently was inevitable – what surprised us was the outcome
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Anamika Sinha

Having recently stepped away from my role as Director of Manovikas Kendra, I find myself with the rare gift of time – to look back, reflect, and make sense of experiences that shaped my practice over the decades. The pandemic years stand out as one such defining chapter.

While this story unfolds in a special education setting, its heartbeat will resonate with every teacher who lived through the early days of the pandemic. The urgency to stay connected with students, the fear of failing them, and the need to relearn everything were experiences we all shared. At our institute, Manovikas Kendra, we weren’t trying to lead a digital revolution. We were only trying to not lose touch with the children we cared about. What followed, surprised even us. If you’ve ever wondered what teaching with heart looks like, we hope this journey speaks to you, no matter where or what you teach.

When COVID-19 forced schools across the country to shut down, the entire education system responded with urgency to develop and adopt alternative educational provisions. Mainstream schools swiftly transitioned to online learning, but for us in the field of special education, the questions ran deeper. Could a screen ever replicate the warmth, patience, and personal connection that our learners with special needs rely on so heavily? Could virtual education truly support children whose learning journeys are rooted in touch, eye contact, structured routines, and one-on-one guidance?

At Manovikas Kendra, a Kolkata-based institute with over five decades of trailblazing work in diagnosis and assessment, therapeutic intervention, special education, rehabilitation, inclusive outreach, and biomedical research, these weren’t abstract concerns. They went straight to the heart of our mission. For our students, school is not just a place of learning; it’s a second home, a safe space where predictability, sensory engagement, and emotional security are woven into every timetable. Shifting to a digital mode felt almost like asking a plant to bloom without sunlight. But, we knew we had to try.

Stepping into the unknown

Our students have a wide range of support needs. Many struggle with poor eye contact, withdrawn behaviour, fleeting attention spans, or require consistent physical and verbal prompting to remain engaged. Some children may exhibit hyperactivity or have difficulty remaining seated for extended periods, while others may display challenging behaviours that are difficult to manage even within a structured environment. These difficulties added to the complexity of shifting to a digital mode of instruction.

Uncertainty blanketed everything. No one knew when the lockdown would lift or when we could meet our students in person again. Panic and disappointment were in the air, and for parents of children with special needs, the situation was particularly harrowing. How could they explain to their child why they couldn’t go to school? Why had friends, teachers, and therapists disappeared overnight?

We also had to prepare the children and young adults for COVID-19 safety measures like using sanitizer, washing hands frequently, wearing masks, and maintaining a safe distance. This wasn’t easy, especially for our learners on the Autism Spectrum. For them, masks were not just uncomfortable but distressing. Many resisted initially, overwhelmed by the sensory discomfort. Though, with time, modelling, visual cues, and gentle persistence from both school and home, they slowly began to accept this new routine. Their progress was quiet but powerful, a reflection of their resilience and our collective trust.

As the days turned into weeks, behavioural issues began surfacing in many children. Idle, confused, and missing the familiarity of their school routines, they struggled with restlessness and emotional outbursts. Parents, too, were overwhelmed. Domestic help was unavailable, external support systems had collapsed, and the demands of caregiving had intensified. They were expected to be caregivers, therapists, and teachers, all at once, and often while managing their own jobs and fears.

By mid-April 2020, it was clear that we couldn’t simply wait for things to return to “normal.” We needed to find a way to reach our children. So, a staff meeting was held with all our teachers and therapists, virtually, of course. After much discussion, concern, and shared courage, we decided on a transition to online classes. We created a WhatsApp group for each class to share important information, circulars for parents and children, and homework assignments for keeping students meaningfully engaged at home. Moreover, a separate WhatsApp group was created for all 120 members of Manovikas Kendra’s teaching staff to streamline communication. These WhatsApp groups proved to be incredibly effective, working almost like magic.

Our first hesitant steps

Initially, some parents were hesitant to join virtual classes. They felt their children would not be able to concentrate or participate meaningfully. The class teachers personally reached out to these parents, patiently explaining the new format and offering reassurance. Gradually, all families came on board, and the classes saw full participation.

Our first attempts were cautious, shaped more by necessity than confidence. We began with what was most familiar to our parents, WhatsApp video calls. These were short, often interrupted by technical glitches or dropped connections, but they helped re-establish a connection between school and home.

We began with short, two-hour virtual sessions to help children ease into the new mode. As families and students adapted, we slowly extended the schedule, gradually rebuilding a full school day in the online format. This helped preserve the structure and rhythm children needed, without overwhelming them from the outset.

Gradually, we started to explore our rhythm. We reached out to parents individually, conducted online meetings to understand their realities, and began redesigning routines around each child’s Individualized Education Plan (IEP). Some of our teachers had never used digital tools before. Yet, they stepped up, learning new apps, recording videos, conducting sessions from their homes, and even creating custom teaching aids with whatever resources they had on hand.

In-house training sessions became frequent. We learned from one another, supported each other, and grew together.

Learning to teach all over again

Very soon, we realized that online education could not and should not aim to replicate in-person learning. Instead, it needed to become something entirely new.

For many children, the sudden interruption of sensory integration, physiotherapy, and other therapeutic services had a significant impact. These are not add-ons; they are central to their regulation, communication, and functional progress. Parents began noticing signs of regression and shared their concerns with us. We knew we had to act quickly. We prioritized starting therapy in every possible form, from online demonstrations and home plans to live tele-therapy sessions, ensuring that no child missed out on these vital systems of support.

We adapted therapy sessions, including speech, physiotherapy, occupational, and behavioural therapies for online delivery, guiding parents to implement the therapies at home. We also included co-curricular activities like music, yoga, art, dance, storytelling, and even non-fire cooking to bring joy and diversity to the week. Teachers recorded simple videos using household items, and parents responded by sending back photos and clips of their child’s work.

We gently encouraged familiar rituals, wearing school uniforms during class, packing their school bags, and setting up a learning corner at home. These small steps helped preserve a sense of identity and routine.

This phase was not without challenges. Technological barriers were real. Some families struggled with unstable internet services, limited devices, or a lack of digital knowledge. But what stood out most was the resilience of our community. Despite fatigue, fear, and frustration, families stayed with us. They believed in the effort, and that belief was our biggest strength.

Meeting hurdles with heart

For some students and young adults with severe behavioural challenges, our school counsellors had to intervene actively. They offered regular consultations and crisis support to both children and their families, often beyond regular school hours, to help manage outbursts, anxiety, and emotional distress. These efforts were instrumental in ensuring continuity and care, especially for our most vulnerable learners.

The road was far from smooth. Our learners, having sensory and attention challenges, struggled with the demands of screens. We had to rethink everything: shorter, more flexible sessions; creative use of props and gestures; stories and songs to enhance a child’s attention through a screen.
