The digital divide made its presence felt. Not all families had access to reliable internet or smart devices. We responded with care and creativity, sharing audio instructions, sending printable worksheets, and even following up with phone calls so that no child was left behind.

In our rural branch, the challenges deepened. Our institute provided smartphones to some families. But many children were first-generation learners, and parents were unfamiliar with digital tools. Here, siblings became co-teachers, helping parents navigate the new world of online learning. Language barriers added another layer, but our teachers didn’t just circulate messages; they patiently explained instructions over calls in the families’ languages.

Virtual learning would not have succeeded without the wholehearted involvement of parents. They became active partners, assisting during classes, supporting therapy sessions, and creating a learning-friendly environment at home. Many parents assisted with speech and occupational exercises, shared photos and videos of progress, and joined in music, yoga, or art activities. Their patience, adaptability, and emotional support transformed homes into classrooms, making every small success possible.

Surprises along the way

One moment that remains etched in our memory happened during an online music class. A child who normally needed one-on-one prompting suddenly sang along on screen, unmuted, smiling brightly as clapping emojis from classmates filled the chat box. It was such a simple moment, yet it meant everything.

Teachers uncovered talents they never knew they had, designing interactive visuals, crafting engaging screen-based tasks, and using digital games and music to engage students. Our staff transformed into digital educators almost overnight, driven not by formal training but by unwavering commitment and compassion. Every celebration, from the Monsoon Festival and Independence Day to the Annual Sports Day, was brought to life on virtual platforms, with all the joy and enthusiasm intact.

Connections beyond the classroom

One unexpected gift of the virtual transition was the opportunity to connect with a wider learning community. Young students from various professional courses joined us virtually as interns and volunteers, bringing fresh, innovative ideas. It became a mutually enriching experience. We also began organizing webinars and interactive sessions with experts from India and abroad, opportunities that had previously felt too expensive or logistically challenging to pursue.

These engagements enriched our work, offered our teachers fresh ideas, and provided families with much-needed validation and support. We also began involving grandparents and siblings in sessions, strengthening the circle of care around each child.

Importantly, the digital exposure helped many of our children become more tech-savvy, a skill that is crucial in today’s world, especially for building future independence and inclusion.

What we have taken forward

As we returned to in-person schooling, we carried many of these virtual learnings with us. Today, we offer hybrid options for outstation families, share digital resources to reinforce learning at home, and conduct remote consultations for those who can’t always travel. We have realized that special education is not a place, it’s a philosophy. It is rooted in believing in every child’s potential, adapting to their needs, partnering with families, and staying open to change. The pandemic didn’t break us, it reshaped us.

A closing thought for all educators

Our journey through virtual education may have started in a special school. Yet it taught us something much broader: the essence of teaching lies not in methods or platforms, but in the relationships we nurture and the belief we hold in every child’s potential.

We learned that connection matters more than content. That patience, presence, and creativity can turn even the most unfamiliar circumstances into meaningful learning moments. When schools and families come together with empathy and trust, incredible things are possible.

Teaching differently was inevitable. But what moved us deeply, and what we carry forward, is the reminder that every child, regardless of ability, thrives when they are seen, heard, and valued. That’s not just a special educator’s lesson. It’s a humane one. And it belongs in every classroom.

Dr. Anamika Sinha is an RCI-registered rehabilitation professional with over 30 years of experience in disability and inclusive education. She is currently a Consultant at Fortis Healthcare Ltd., Kolkata, and continues to work as an independent professional in the field. She formerly served as the Director of Manovikas Kendra, Kolkata, a 50-year-old institute dedicated to the field of disability and special education. She can be reached at anasinha2014@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Anamika Sinha
From the initial hesitant WhatsApp calls to full-fledged virtual schooling, Anamika Sinha captures how collective resilience sustained learning during COVID-19.

item.title: Eco conversations in Tamarind Grove
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Nandini Dholepat

“Namaste, how are you doing? I am Sudheer. I am a doctor practicing Ayurveda here in Nallur village. Prof. Prabhakar was my mentor in college,” Sudheer introduced himself.

Everyone greeted Dr. Sudheer and told him how much they enjoyed the tamarind juice he had sent them.

“Can you tell us how to make the juice?” Sujatha asked.

“In Ayurveda, it is called chinchadi paanaka. Chincha means tamarind in Sanskrit. It is easy to make. Soak 2-3 well-formed, ripe tamarind beans in water for two hours. Fry a spoonful of jeera and 3-4 black peppercorns on a low flame for 2-3 minutes, powder it. Mash the tamarind and filter. Add approximately 100 ml of water and mix with the jeera and pepper powder. Keep it in a cool place for half an hour before you consume. On a summer day, you can have one glass of this juice. It will help cool you down. It is useful in indigestion and is a good appetizer as well,” Sudheer replied.

“What other Ayurvedic formulations are made using tamarind?” Madhavi asked.

“Chinchadi tailam, which is used for external applications for arthritis, paralysis, and neurological problems. Chinchadi lehyam is prepared from a mixture of herbs. Lehyam is a semisolid jam-like preparation. It helps improve digestion. It is used in the treatment of excessive burning sensation, jaundice, anemia, and chronic fever.”

“Sir, do you make this on a large scale? Do you have a patent over this?” a student asked.

“Prof. Prabhakar informed me about your visit. So, I made the juice today, only for you. Otherwise, we advise patients to prepare this to consume during the summer, as tamarind grows in abundance during that time of the year. To answer your second question, this is not my invention to take a patent. Ayurvedic medicines cannot be patented as they are the outcome of traditional knowledge, which does not meet the patentability criteria,” Sudheer replied.

“Oh! Then we have hundreds of Ayurveda, Siddha, and Unani medicines. How do we protect them? I heard that turmeric and neem were patented by Western countries,” Sujatha said surprised.

“Prabhakar sir will answer your questions. I have to attend to patients. I will take your leave now,” Sudheer hurried away.

Everyone thanked Sudheer.

Prabhakar replied, “A patent helps you protect your inventions. It gives you exclusive rights to make, use, and sell your invention. Anything novel, inventive, and industrially applicable can be patented. But, in the case of traditional knowledge, it is knowledge transferred from generation to generation. It is difficult to trace the inventor because it is already being practiced in society.”

“Then, how to protect traditional knowledge? My grandfather and uncles are weavers. Weaving is also traditional knowledge. How to protect their designs?” a student asked.

‘You are right, weaving, carpentry, jewellery-making, agricultural practices, folk music, performing arts, all fall under traditional knowledge. Under intellectual property, weaving patterns can be registered under “Design or Geographical Indications.” Similarly, carpentry, pottery, jewellery-making, as well. In general, agricultural practices and methods of teaching cannot be patented. Music composition and literary work can be protected under copyright, similarly, recorded versions of the performing arts, too,” Prabhakar replied.

“What about trademark?” Madhavi asked.

“A trademark is a name, symbol, or mark that distinguishes a product or brand from other products or brands. By extension, it can also be used to describe something characteristic of a person or thing in a more metaphorical way, such as “the singer’s trademark rhythm.” It is symbolized with an ‘R’ inside a circle as a superscript. Look at the arrow here,” I took out a box of glass colours I had in my bag to show everyone.

“Wow, we find many products marked like this in supermarkets,” Madhavi said.

“Where is the patent office?” a student asked.

“In our country, the office of the Controller General of Patents, Designs and Trademarks is under the Ministry of Commerce and Trade. The head office is in Kolkata, and has branch offices in New Delhi, Mumbai, and Chennai. You can find more information on this website,” said Prabhakar, showing the home page.

“But, what happened to the turmeric and neem patents?” a student asked.

“It was revoked by providing many documentary evidences in both cases. Indian scientists and organizations like the Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR) provided strong evidence to the US Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO) and the European Patent Office (EPO) that the patents were based on pre-existing knowledge,” I replied.

“Now such incidents do not happen. We have a Traditional Knowledge and Digital Library. (TKDL) to protect Indian traditional medicinal knowledge and prevent its misappropriation at International Patent Offices. TKDL integrates diverse systems like Ayurveda, Unani, Siddha, Yoga, and knowledge documented in languages such as Sanskrit, Arabic, Urdu, Persian, Tamil, English, and many others. Currently, TKDL has books on Indian systems of medicine, which are available in the open domain and can be sourced by any individual or organization at the national and international level,” Prabhakar added.

“Let us walk to the temple. It will open by sunset. Walk on the roadside only,” I told everyone.

While walking, Rini pointed, “Look at these trees! They look so beautiful with red and blue flowers. The red flower looks like a cock’s comb. Are those finger like projections fruits?”

“The blue flower tree is Jacaranda. Flowers attract honeybees. It is native to tropical and subtropical regions of America. The red flower tree is Spathodia campanulata, commonly known as the African tulip tree, as it is native to Africa; its nectar is popular with many birds. Yes, the upward projections are fruits. These are commonly grown as avenue trees,” Prabhakar replied.

“These trees are non-native, but naturalized,” Anand said.

“But ma’am, now a lot of developmental activities are happening, like broadening of highways, mining, etc. How to avoid these to save biodiversity? We hear a lot about human-wildlife conflict,” a student asked.

“Before considering any such major programmes, impact assessments are done. Public opinion will also be sought. Compensation will be provided to the local community to find alternative livelihoods. However, challenges are always there. Population is rising day by day, and we have only one Earth. To resolve disputes, the National Green Tribunal has been established under the National Green Tribunal Act 2010 for effective and expeditious disposal of cases relating to environmental protection and conservation of forests and other natural resources. This includes enforcement of any legal right relating to the environment and giving relief and compensation for damages to persons and property. It is a specialized body equipped with the necessary expertise to handle environmental disputes involving multi-disciplinary issues,” I explained.

“Those who have visited National Parks must have seen signboards about restrictions for entry. Each season, the timings to visit these forests change slightly. In some cases, the animal safari is closed during the rainy season,” Prabhakar said.

“Yes sir, Kaziranga closes during the monsoon, generally between May and September,” a student said.

“Bandipur National Park has banned traffic between 9 pm to 6 am,” Anand said.

“We need to respect these guidelines, so that animals also feel comfortable in their habitats,” Madhavi added.

“Ma'am, you told us about a huge tree you saw in Devarakadu. Similarly, we have a huge tree in our village near Mysore. It is a banyan tree belonging to our neighbours. They have been protecting the tree for many generations. My grandfather, Head of the Panchayat, honoured the landlord for his contribution. Because it is on a farmland and a huge portion of the land is under the shade of the tree, cultivation is not possible. But still, they maintain it. Look at these photographs,” a student opened her tab to show the photographs.

I said, “Great, this is how people are encouraged to protect heritage sites and trees. Whenever you go to your village, you can explain the importance of heritage sites and trees to the others.”

Suggested activities

  1. Identify non-native trees growing on the roadside and in parks.
  2. List the dos and don'ts when you enter a national park.

References

  1. https://drnivedhawrites.blogspot.com/2018/04/chincha-panaka-summer-drink.html
  2. https://ipindia.gov.in/
  3. https://www.tkdl.res.in/tkdl/langdefault/common/Home.asp?GL=Eng
  4. https://www.greentribunal.gov.in/about-us

Nandini Dholepat is a botanist who has been involved in teaching and research for more than 25 years. Currently, she is engaged as a consultant for Intellectual Property Rights for Karnataka State Council for Science and Technology, Bengaluru, and is a visiting faculty member at RIWATCH, Roing, Arunachal Pradesh. She can be reached at nandinidholepat@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nandini Dholepat
What begins as a recipe for tamarind juice turns into lessons on traditional knowledge, intellectual property, and environmental stewardship, shares Nandini Dholepat.
